EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain which fell in late January helped to improve the drought conditions over the Gila Region in February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe(D2) drought conditions across the entire zone improved to Moderate(D1) conditions over the eastern half of the zone on February 3rd. The Gila Hot Springs Cooperative Observer reported 0.46 inches of precipitation which was 0.41 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rain at the very end of January into early February brought an improvement in drought conditions for the Gila Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern portions of the zone which started in Severe(D2) drought improved to Moderate(D1) drought on February 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rain at the very end of January into early February brought an improvement in drought conditions for the Gila Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The zone started with the northern and eastern portions in Severe(D2) drought which was put into Moderate(D1) drought on February 3rd. The remainder of the zone was in Moderate(D1) or Abnormally Dry(D0) drought conditions through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals over the Gila Region for January were well above normal, but occurred late in the month. No change was noted in the drought categories during the month, but did occur with early February with the next drought update.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone remained in Severe(D2) drought through the month of January. The Gila Hot Springs Cooperative Observer reported 2.21 inches of precipitation which was 1.27 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals over the Gila Region for January were well above normal, but occurred late in the month. No change was noted in the drought categories during the month, but did occur with early February with the next drought update.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern half of the zone was in Severe(D2) drought during the month of January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals over the Gila Region for January were well above normal, but occurred late in the month. No change was noted in the drought categories during the month, but did occur with early February with the next drought update.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern and eastern portions of the zone remained in Severe(D2) drought during the month of January. The Redrock Cooperative Observer reported 2.51 inches of precipitation which was 1.65 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A positively tilted upper trough was dropping through the Four Corners region. At the same time, a strong surface high extending from the Great Basin into the central high plains was bringing in cold surface temperatures from the northeast. These features combined to create a band of heavy snow from eastern Luna County into northern Otero County where up to 9 inches of snow was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported 5.3 inches of snow about 4 miles north of Akela.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level trough moving over the region resulted in heavy snowfall across portions of southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow reported in Hope.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of light to moderate snow, and freezing drizzle, occurred across southeast New Mexico.  the wintry precipitation was produced ahead of a slow moving upper trough while an arctic airmass resided over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snowfall reported in Tatum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow fell on southeast New Mexico and west Texas as an arctic airmass resided over the region, and ahead of a slow moving upper trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow in Carlsbad, on top of additional ice accumulation from earlier in the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was moving past the Rocky Mountains into the plains with an upper level disturbance over the Arizona/New Mexico border.  There was an axis of moisture that was moving over New Mexico.  These conditions, combined with good instability, allowed for thunderstorms to develop across southeast New Mexico.

There was good moisture across southeast New Mexico and the Trans Pecos. This moisture along with daytime heating created a good environment for thunderstorms to develop as a frontal boundary moved into the area through the evening hours.  Thunderstorms developed along and behind this frontal boundary that produced hail and damaging winds.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent spring storm system that developed over the Pacific Northwest dove southeast toward Arizona on the 25th and pushed very slowly east across New Mexico through the 27th. Widespread rain and high elevation snow developed during the predawn hours on the 26th over central and western New Mexico then over eastern New Mexico late on the 26th through the 27th. Many reports of heavy rain and small hail were received around the state with the convective activity on the 26th. A cold front then moved southwest over the east during the evening on the 26th and provided lift for widespread rain and very heavy, wet mountain snow. Locations across the plains picked up to 1 to 2 inches of rain, while the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo mountains were pounded with 12 to 18 inches of snow. Some power outages were reported around Angel Fire. A cold air funnel was even spotter in the Estancia Valley west of Moriarty.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter and CoCoRaHS reports indicated between 9 and 15 inches fell along the higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly progressed east across the southwest US and it provided upper level support for thunderstorms to develop.  Good moisture was present east of a dryline that was backed up to the Guadalupe Mountains. Some locations had low to mid 60 dewpoints.  High instability and strong shear allowed for thunderstorms to become organized and produce large hail and damaging winds across southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm moved across Eddy County and produced an estimated wind gust of 60 mph and knocked down 3 power poles and a road sign along US 285 3 miles northwest of Carlsbad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of rich subtropical moisture surged northeast over New Mexico while a cold front moved south out of Colorado. Very warm temperatures at the beginning of the event forced snow levels above 9,000 feet through the day on the 19th. As the colder air filtered in from the north, snow levels fell and very heavy, wet snow accumulations occurred over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. SNOTEL and ski resort locations above 8,000 feet reported total snow accumulations on the order of 12 to 18 inches by the morning of the 20th. A few severe storms developed along the cold front during the early evening hours, dropping pea to quarter sized hail. No significant impacts to travel were noted due to the wetter nature of the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent back door cold front oozed southwest across northeastern New Mexico late on the 3rd and into the 4th. Shallow moisture associated with the front interacted with a weak upper level low pressure system that approached from the west. Very cold temperatures with this system along with gusty winds created severe driving conditions across many areas of northeastern New Mexico where dangerous black ice was reported. The greatest snowfall accumulations occurred in upslope areas of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains where generally 3 to 6 inches was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters and public reports indicated between 3 and 8 inches of snowfall over the area with bitterly cold temperatures and hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm event is a continuation of the epic snowfall event that began on February 24th. The impressive fetch of subtropical moisture that streamed northeast out of the eastern Pacific during the end of February continued into early March. The area around Chama eastward into the nearby San Juan Mountains reported between 3 and 5 feet of snowfall over the 8-day period. The heaviest snowfalls during this stretch were nearly 18 inches from February 22nd to the 23rd and 33 inches on February 28th and March 1st. This event made it into the record books on several days for Chama. Surprisingly, no major structural issues and extreme travel conditions were noted around the area during this period. The Sangre de Cristo Mountains received huge amounts of snowfall as well through the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP, CoCoRaHS, and SNOTEL sites reported between 3 and 5 feet of snowfall over the area during this extended heavy snowfall event. The storm total of 59 inches for the 8-day period was the 11th heaviest event on record dating back to 1893. The 33 inches reported by the Chama COOP observer for the 2-day period ending March 1st was the 7th heaviest snowfall on record. Several other daily records were also achieved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over southern Arizona moving southward, and this feature increased upper level lift over West Texas and southeast New Mexico.  This upper low was pulling moisture northward into the area from the Gulf of Mexico.  These conditions contributed to severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds as well as flash flooding developing across the area.  Thunderstorms increased in coverage overnight as an upper level disturbance moved over the region.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Lea County and produced flash flooding in Jal.  The Jal Police Department reported that three roads were blocked off due to two to three feet of water over them.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance combined with a strengthening low level jet over eastern New Mexico generated numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across the plains during the late evening hours of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. A couple severe storms produced 60 mph wind gusts in De Baca County and half dollar size hail in Roosevelt County. An extensive band of thunderstorms impacted the area from northern Chaves County northeastward into De Baca and Quay counties. Training storm cells produced torrential rainfall and flash flooding in portions of the area. The storms dropped anywhere from 1 inch to nearly 5 inches of rainfall over the east central plains, with the heaviest totals falling in De Baca, Quay, and Curry counties. The heavy rainfall led to 3 reported road closures while causing Yeso Creek near Fort Sumner to overflow its banks. No other significant damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 3.5 inches of rainfall in less than 24 hours near Tolar. No official reports of flash flooding were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The wet May pattern continued as yet another round of scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over central New Mexico. This activity produced gusty and erratic outflow winds along with heavy rain and small hail across the Albuquerque Metro Area during the afternoon hours. An unsecured cherry picker crane positioned atop a flat bed truck was toppled by an outflow wind gusts estimated at 30 to 40 mph. The operator of the crane and one child were killed in the accident and several other injuries were reported. A second child passed away from the incident several days later while receiving treatment in the hospital.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cherry picker crane carrying several individuals at Cochiti Road and Dorothy Street was toppled by an erratic outflow wind gust between 30 and 40 mph. The crane operator and one child were killed on the scene. Three others were injured. One of the injured children died several days later in the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry, windy and warm conditions dominated the first 10 days of April 2015. An impressive rain event then unfolded across the southeast third of the state on the 12th and 13th. Numerous one to two inch rainfall amounts were reported across Lincoln, Chaves and Roosevelt counties. A minor to moderate snow event helped to bring some precipitation to the north on the 18th. A welcomed, widespread rain and high northern mountain snow event impacted the state from the 26th into the 28th. The month ended mostly dry and warm.

An area of enhanced lift aloft helped to generate high-based showers and thunderstorms late on the 21st into the early morning hours of the 22nd. One thunderstorm produced a strong dry microburst, which resulted in a 62 mph gust with no reported rain.  A few other thunderstorms produced gusts up to 50 mph, but nothing more.  No damage was reported due to this gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS measured a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph.

SNOTEL and ski resort locations above 8,000 feet reported total snow accumulations on the order of 12 to 18 inches by the morning of the 20th. A few severe storms developed along the cold front during the early evening hours, dropping pea to quarter sized hail. No significant impacts to travel were noted due to the wetter nature of the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ski sites and SNOTELs reported between 9 and 19 inches of wet snow. Intense snowfall rates occurred with this snow event during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance combined with a strengthening low level jet over eastern New Mexico generated numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across the plains during the late evening hours of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. A couple severe storms produced 60 mph wind gusts in De Baca County and half dollar size hail in Roosevelt County. An extensive band of thunderstorms impacted the area from northern Chaves County northeastward into De Baca and Quay counties. Training storm cells produced torrential rainfall and flash flooding in portions of the area. The storms dropped anywhere from 1 inch to nearly 5 inches of rainfall over the east central plains, with the heaviest totals falling in De Baca, Quay, and Curry counties. The heavy rainfall led to 3 reported road closures while causing Yeso Creek near Fort Sumner to overflow its banks. No other significant damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A schoolnet site in House reported a 1-day total rainfall of 3.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late season winter storm system dropped south from west central Colorado late on the 15th into the 16th. A strong upper level jet combined with a weak lee side trough to produce strong winds during the late morning and early afternoon hours on the 16th across south central New Mexico.  Observed gusts between 40 and 55 mph were common while one site reported gusts up to 64 mph.  No damage due to wind was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site on top of Oscura Peak measured multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph, peaking at 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough ejecting northeastward through New Mexico resulted in strong winds in the Guadalupe Mountains.

Strong southeast low level flow over the region forced abundant moisture all the way westward through the Rio Grande Valley and onto the Continental Divide. Strong thunderstorms developed first over the Albuquerque Metro and east mountain communities before pushing east into the high plains during the late afternoon. Supercell thunderstorms and multicell clusters exploded over the plains and produced large hail and high winds over Lincoln, De Baca, Curry, Roosevelt, and Chaves counties. The largest hail stones up to tennis ball size were observed, as well as wind gusts up to 65 mph. Flash flooding was also reported around Portales where soils were already saturated by weeks of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated easily 60 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms drifted northeast across far northeastern New Mexico. These storms produced several rounds of hail around Raton and numerous funnel clouds from Raton to near Des Moines. One particularly strong storm produced accumulations of dime to penny size hail in Raton. Heavy rainfall was also reported with this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of a funnel cloud were received with a strong thunderstorm near Des Moines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of Gulf of Mexico moisture surged northwest during the early morning while a weak upper level trough drifted east over central New Mexico. A sharp dryline over far eastern New Mexico set the stage for strong to severe thunderstorms by mid to late afternoon. These storms produced large hail across portions of the area, including Union, Curry and Roosevelt counties. The largest stones fell around Sedan and Portales where golf ball to hen egg size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May 2015 was a very wet month for many areas of New Mexico,  particularly the eastern plains where record or near-record rainfall was observed.  Most areas experienced precipitation totals that were 200-400 percent of normal.  Even locales across central and western New Mexico experienced one of the top ten wettest Mays on record.   This resulted in significant improvement in drought status nearly area-wide, but especially across central and eastern New Mexico.  In fact, moderate to severe drought was completely removed from the eastern plains for the first time in at least five years.  Reservoir storage was below capacity at nearly all lakes and reservoirs at the beginning of the month, with modest to significant improvements by month's end especially those on the Canadian and Pecos Rivers.   Severe or worse drought conditions covered about 12 percent of the state,  as compared to 85 percent for the same period in 2014.

An extensive band of thunderstorms impacted the area from northern Chaves County northeastward into De Baca and Quay counties. Training storm cells produced torrential rainfall and flash flooding in portions of the area. The storms dropped anywhere from 1 inch to nearly 5 inches of rainfall over the east central plains, with the heaviest totals falling in De Baca, Quay, and Curry counties. The heavy rainfall led to 3 reported road closures while causing Yeso Creek near Fort Sumner to overflow its banks. No other significant damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The New Mexico Department of Transportation closed state road 272 at mile marker 9 due to flooding.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET/RED RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system lifted northwest out of southern California on the 4th, setting off widespread showers and thunderstorms across New Mexico. The strongest storms developed along and east of the central mountains where a deep pool of moisture and sufficient instability existed. These storms produced anywhere from pea to quarter sized hail along with heavy rains. A train of storms over far eastern New Mexico resulted in rainfall amounts anywhere from an inch to over 5 inches, with 5.4 inches reported at the Cannon Air Force Base in less than 24 hours. The 5.4 inches is likely the heaviest 24-hour total rainfall for May in the Clovis area dating back to 1911, with the previous record of 3.45 inches at the Clovis airport. The all-time 24-hour rainfall record for Clovis is 5.26 inches set on September 7, 1957. This heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding within the base and in nearby Clovis through the early morning hours on the 5th. Home evacuations and submerged cars resulted from the flash flooding. No injuries or fatalities resulted from the flooding or hail. Temperatures were cold enough farther north in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains where several inches of snow was reported overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Impressive late season snowfall amounts between 2 and 7 inches were reported over the higher terrain of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. No significant impacts were noted.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET/RED RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The slow-moving upper level storm system responsible for isolated strong thunderstorms over New Mexico on the 14th edged closer to New Mexico on the 15th. A large subtropical moisture tap and associated upper level jet core ahead of this system moved northeast across eastern New Mexico during the late afternoon and evening hours. Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved northeast over the region, some of which became strong to severe. Quarter size hail was reported at Pep. Unfortunately, a woman traveling to Ruidoso from Albuquerque was struck by lightning while on a motorcycle east of Carrizozo. She died several days later in the hospital. Meanwhile, farther north, enough cold air with the approaching upper level storm system allowed snow to accumulate over the northern mountains. Many high elevation areas in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains reported 2 to 12 inches of wet snow, however impacts were limited due to the very high elevation and relatively warm surface temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Late season heavy snowfall of 2 to 12 inches reported from high elevation SNOTEL sites in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. No impacts were noted due to the very high elevation and relatively warm surface temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southeast upslope flow combined with an upper level disturbance within southwest flow to create widespread showers and thunderstorms across New Mexico. Strong instability over the eastern plains led to a few severe thunderstorms with up to quarter size hail during the afternoon and overnight hours. Local officials evacuated 33 families from those homes and put them in hotel lodging. The flooding in Clovis forced the closure of U.S. Highway 60/84. Approximately 40 feet of state highway 108 between mile markers 6 and 10 was washed out. Clovis police had to use an armored vehicle to rescue 7 victims stranded by or caught in fast moving waters or flooded streets.

A strong easterly push of low level moisture over eastern New Mexico interacted with an upper level trough ejecting out of the southern Rockies to produce numerous showers and thunderstorms over the eastern plains. Strong to severe thunderstorms erupted along the dryline over the high plains and moved southeast into eastern New Mexico. Several reports of large hail were received from Colfax County southeast to De Baca and Roosevelt counties. Heavy rainfall falling on saturated grounds within Clovis produced urban flooding of low lying areas and playa lakes. The strongest storms rolled through Roosevelt County where golf ball size hail and even a tornado was reported around Milnesand. No damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of elephant trunk tornado on the ground between Milnesand and Lingo. No damage or injuries reported.

A backdoor cold front along with northerly flow aloft boosted moisture across northern New Mexico on the 27th. A disturbance within that northerly flow kicked off early morning showers and thunderstorms along and east of the central mountains, moving slowly to the south. Rainfall amounts between half an inch and an inch were common with these storms, with up to 2.2 inches recorded in the Ute Park area. Heavy rains between Eagle Nest and Cimarron caused localized flash flooding that led to 1 fatality at Philmont Scout Ranch and the closure of US Highway 64 for nearly 36 hours. A second round of storms developed by the early afternoon, mainly along and west of the central mountain chain. A couple storms became severe near Jemez Springs and Sandia Park, producing up to half dollar size hail. The severe storm south of Jemez Springs also produced flash flooding that closed state road 485 and forest service road 376 as well as disrupting traffic at low water crossings along state road 4. Major damage was reported to state road 485 from the flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chaser reported lots of hail covering the ground with the largest stone around half dollar size.

This feature helped set off strong to severe early morning thunderstorms across parts of the eastern plains, moving north northeast. Hail reports from these thunderstorms were generally pea to half inch in size, but the largest report was penny size southeast of Tucumcari, New Mexico. No damage resulted from the hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter woken by penny size hail that fell during the early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved out of central Arizona and into northern Colorado during the day on the 5th ushering colder air over New Mexico. Residual moisture and a weak dryline along the eastern plains resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms across the southeast and far eastern plains. These storms produced strong winds, pea to penny size hail, and brief periods of heavy rain. The combination of weak shear and cold air aloft caused cold-air funnel clouds to form in association with some of the stronger storms, notably near Roswell.  It was in Roswell that reports indicated multiple funnel clouds developed over different parts of the city. One funnel cloud briefly touched down on the southwest side of the city, damaging two outbuildings during it's short-lived life as a tornado. No injuries or fatalities were reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported penny sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance combined with a strengthening low level jet over eastern New Mexico generated numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across the plains during the late evening hours of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. A couple severe storms produced 60 mph wind gusts in De Baca County and half dollar size hail in Roosevelt County. An extensive band of thunderstorms impacted the area from northern Chaves County northeastward into De Baca and Quay counties. Training storm cells produced torrential rainfall and flash flooding in portions of the area. The storms dropped anywhere from 1 inch to nearly 5 inches of rainfall over the east central plains, with the heaviest totals falling in De Baca, Quay, and Curry counties. The heavy rainfall led to 3 reported road closures while causing Yeso Creek near Fort Sumner to overflow its banks. No other significant damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter measured 4.9 inches of rain over the course of 3 hours. This resulted in fast flowing water in their yard, with the highest watermark on the side of their house being 8 inches. No water was reported to have entered the home. The end time of the flooding and areal coverage were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 2015 continued to be very active across the state, especially the northeast plateau and portions of the lower Rio Grande valley and eastern plains. The Four Corners region saw very wet conditions during the first half of the month with many sites reporting 300 to 400 percent of normal. This combined with a wet May knocked drought conditions back even more. Moderate to severe drought conditions were alleviated around the San Juan Mountains, portions of the Continental Divide, and between Gallup and Farmington. Near to above normal precipitation across the remainder of the area continued to keep drought from returning during June. Reservoir storage was still way below capacity at nearly all lakes and reservoirs through the month, with the exception of a few locations along the Canadian and Pecos basins. Severe or worse drought conditions by the end of the month covered nearly 12 percent of the state, as compared to 85 percent for the same period in 2014.

A dryline extended from near Roswell to Tucumcari and served to focus a few late afternoon thunderstorms across eastern New Mexico. The strongest storms impacted the area along Interstate 40 near San Jon where penny to nickel size hail and heavy rain were reported. Localized wind damage was also reported to some electrical poles near Rosebud. Powerful thunderstorms were also noted in the area from Roswell to Fort Sumner, House, and Tucumcari during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southeast low level flow over the region forced abundant moisture all the way westward through the Rio Grande Valley and onto the Continental Divide. Strong thunderstorms developed first over the Albuquerque Metro and east mountain communities before pushing east into the high plains during the late afternoon. Supercell thunderstorms and multicell clusters exploded over the plains and produced large hail and high winds over Lincoln, De Baca, Curry, Roosevelt, and Chaves counties. The largest hail stones up to tennis ball size were observed, as well as wind gusts up to 65 mph. Flash flooding was also reported around Portales where soils were already saturated by weeks of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intersection of 17th and 18th streets with 3rd and Kilgor flooding. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper low was located over Arizona with a surface low and trough near the Rio Grande Valley. Deep moisture was located east of the surface low with dew points well into the 50s. Isolated, severe thunderstorms developed over the Sacramento Mountains with golf ball size hail reported in Weed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The person that reported golf ball size hail in Weed said it appeared to be more extensive toward the town of Sacramento.

Reports indicated that one cold air funnel cloud briefly made contact with the ground and damaged the roof and walls of two outbuildings. No injuries or fatalities were reported with this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance combined with a weak backdoor cold front initiated thunderstorms along the eastern slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on May 1st.  One storm became severe as it moved east along the frontal boundary, dropping mainly pea size hail, though some hailstones were slightly larger than a quarter.  The hail fell at the Capulin National Monument before the storm weakened as it continued to move east.  No damage was reported as result of the hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Capulin National Monument shared photos of hail slightly larger than quarter size mixed in the mainly pea size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shower and thunderstorm coverage decreased considerably over the vast of majority of New Mexico this day as compared to the active weather pattern the previous several days. The weather did, however, remain active over the far southeast corner of the state where the dryline continued to focus more severe thunderstorms. A broken line of thunderstorms developed from near Roswell northeast to Tucumcari during the late afternoon hours and slowly moved southeast into west Texas through the evening. This line of storms prompted numerous severe thunderstorm warnings and flash flood warnings.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over eastern Roosevelt County where Doppler radar indicated between 4 and 6 inches of rain had fallen. State police reported severe flooding along State Road 114.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public spotter reported nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated slow-moving showers and thunderstorms developed over the high terrain of central and northern New Mexico and produced heavy rainfall, hail, and strong winds. The strongest storm developed over the northern end of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and shifted into the Vermejo Park area. Half dollar size hail produced some damage to vehicles and broke a window in a nearby building. Another storm impacted Taos where penny size hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail estimated at half dollar size caused dented hoods on trucks and broke a window in a building.

Showers and thunderstorms developed along the mountains of central New Mexico and moved northeast across the plains. Quarter size hail was reported along Interstate 25 near Maxwell and some wind damage was reported to trees around Roswell. Several other very strong storms were noted however no severe reports were obtained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NSSL SHAVE passed along report of quarter size hail near Maxwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May 2015 was a very wet month for many areas of New Mexico,  particularly the eastern plains where record or near-record rainfall was observed.  Most areas experienced precipitation totals that were 200-400 percent of normal.  Even locales across central and western New Mexico experienced one of the top ten wettest Mays on record.   This resulted in significant improvement in drought status nearly area-wide, but especially across central and eastern New Mexico.  In fact, moderate to severe drought was completely removed from the eastern plains for the first time in at least five years.  Reservoir storage was below capacity at nearly all lakes and reservoirs at the beginning of the month, with modest to significant improvements by month's end especially those on the Canadian and Pecos Rivers.   Severe or worse drought conditions covered about 12 percent of the state,  as compared to 85 percent for the same period in 2014.

A fast-moving upper level disturbance moved over southeast New Mexico during the morning of May 8th.  Strong to severe thunderstorms quickly developed over Roosevelt and Curry counties ahead of this atmospheric wave due to a moist and unstable environment near the surface. The storms only lasted about an hour in New Mexico before moving into the Texas panhandle, but the strongest storm managed to produce golf ball size hail near Dora, New Mexico. No damage resulted from the hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public pictures shared on facebook show the golf ball sized hail that fell near Dora.  No reported damage due to the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 2015 continued to be very active across the state, especially the northeast plateau and portions of the lower Rio Grande valley and eastern plains. The Four Corners region saw very wet conditions during the first half of the month with many sites reporting 300 to 400 percent of normal. This combined with a wet May knocked drought conditions back even more. Moderate to severe drought conditions were alleviated around the San Juan Mountains, portions of the Continental Divide, and between Gallup and Farmington. Near to above normal precipitation across the remainder of the area continued to keep drought from returning during June. Reservoir storage was still way below capacity at nearly all lakes and reservoirs through the month, with the exception of a few locations along the Canadian and Pecos basins. Severe or worse drought conditions by the end of the month covered nearly 12 percent of the state, as compared to 85 percent for the same period in 2014.

An upper level trough quickly moved east over New Mexico and interacted with abundant low level moisture and instability over the eastern plains to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms. Most of the storms produced pea to dime size hail as they pushed southeast across the area. Dime size hail covered the ground in Abbott up to 2 inches deep. One of the stronger storms in Guadalupe County produced nickel size hail near Newkirk shortly before 5pm. Slight wind damage was reported around the McDonald's in Santa Rosa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inch diameter tree limbs downed. Small electrical pole toppled. Some siding stripped off roof of McDonalds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shower and thunderstorm coverage decreased considerably over the vast of majority of New Mexico this day as compared to the active weather pattern the previous several days. The weather did, however, remain active over the far southeast corner of the state where the dryline continued to focus more severe thunderstorms. A broken line of thunderstorms developed from near Roswell northeast to Tucumcari during the late afternoon hours and slowly moved southeast into west Texas through the evening. This line of storms prompted numerous severe thunderstorm warnings and flash flood warnings.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over eastern Roosevelt County where Doppler radar indicated between 4 and 6 inches of rain had fallen. State police reported severe flooding along State Road 114.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Roswell Airport recorded a thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated slow-moving showers and thunderstorms developed over the high terrain of central and northern New Mexico and produced heavy rainfall, hail, and strong winds. The strongest storm developed over the northern end of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and shifted into the Vermejo Park area. Half dollar size hail produced some damage to vehicles and broke a window in a nearby building. Another storm impacted Taos where penny size hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed over the northern high terrain of New Mexico once again on the 17th. A severe thunderstorm moved over Sugarite Canyon State Park and dumped quarter size hail on the area. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sugarite Canyon State Park reported dime to quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The focus for showers and thunderstorms shifted into the high terrain of Catron, Socorro, and Lincoln counties during the afternoon of the 19th. These slow-moving thunderstorms produced hail, strong winds, and very heavy rainfall. One storm in particular wreaked havoc on the Red Canyon prescribed burn area where 2 inches of dime to quarter size hail and very heavy rainfall was reported. Another strong thunderstorm impacted Magdalena with nickel size hail. More storms over the White Sands Missile Range area produced minor flooding and wind gusts up to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: USFS reported up to 2 inches of dime to quarter size hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public spotter reported pea to nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level storm system centered over the Great Basin forced moist and unstable southerly flow over New Mexico. A couple of thunderstorms developed during the heat of the afternoon and progressed north-northeast over the eastern plains as well as the Four Corners region. A particularly strong thunderstorm that moved from San Juan County into extreme northwestern Rio Arriba County produced gusty winds and nickel size hail at a recreational vehicle park near Navajo Dam. Leaves were stripped off trees and a couple dead branches broken from the wind and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail stripped leaves off trees at a recreational vehicle park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance combined with a strengthening low level jet over eastern New Mexico generated numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across the plains during the late evening hours of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. A couple severe storms produced 60 mph wind gusts in De Baca County and half dollar size hail in Roosevelt County. An extensive band of thunderstorms impacted the area from northern Chaves County northeastward into De Baca and Quay counties. Training storm cells produced torrential rainfall and flash flooding in portions of the area. The storms dropped anywhere from 1 inch to nearly 5 inches of rainfall over the east central plains, with the heaviest totals falling in De Baca, Quay, and Curry counties. The heavy rainfall led to 3 reported road closures while causing Yeso Creek near Fort Sumner to overflow its banks. No other significant damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter noted that Yeso Creek had overflowed its banks and was also running rapidly. No further information or damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance combined with a strengthening low level jet over eastern New Mexico generated numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across the plains during the late evening hours of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. A couple severe storms produced 60 mph wind gusts in De Baca County and half dollar size hail in Roosevelt County. An extensive band of thunderstorms impacted the area from northern Chaves County northeastward into De Baca and Quay counties. Training storm cells produced torrential rainfall and flash flooding in portions of the area. The storms dropped anywhere from 1 inch to nearly 5 inches of rainfall over the east central plains, with the heaviest totals falling in De Baca, Quay, and Curry counties. The heavy rainfall led to 3 reported road closures while causing Yeso Creek near Fort Sumner to overflow its banks. No other significant damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported several arroyos were overflowing their banks and washing out numerous roads. State Road 294 was closed due to flooding near junction with U.S. Highway 60 for several hours after causative event. Most of the flooding subsided by 3 am which allowed some roads to become passable. No damage was reported with this flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system lifted northwest out of southern California on the 4th, setting off widespread showers and thunderstorms across New Mexico. The strongest storms developed along and east of the central mountains where a deep pool of moisture and sufficient instability existed. These storms produced anywhere from pea to quarter sized hail along with heavy rains. A train of storms over far eastern New Mexico resulted in rainfall amounts anywhere from an inch to over 5 inches, with 5.4 inches reported at the Cannon Air Force Base in less than 24 hours. The 5.4 inches is likely the heaviest 24-hour total rainfall for May in the Clovis area dating back to 1911, with the previous record of 3.45 inches at the Clovis airport. The all-time 24-hour rainfall record for Clovis is 5.26 inches set on September 7, 1957. This heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding within the base and in nearby Clovis through the early morning hours on the 5th. Home evacuations and submerged cars resulted from the flash flooding. No injuries or fatalities resulted from the flooding or hail. Temperatures were cold enough farther north in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains where several inches of snow was reported overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter size hail.

Strong southeast low level flow over the region forced abundant moisture all the way westward through the Rio Grande Valley and onto the Continental Divide. Strong thunderstorms developed first over the Albuquerque Metro and east mountain communities before pushing east into the high plains during the late afternoon. Supercell thunderstorms and multicell clusters exploded over the plains and produced large hail and high winds over Lincoln, De Baca, Curry, Roosevelt, and Chaves counties. The largest hail stones up to tennis ball size were observed, as well as wind gusts up to 65 mph. Flash flooding was also reported around Portales where soils were already saturated by weeks of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported strong wind gust seen all year. No damaged reported but lots of tumbleweeds and dirt blew around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two isolated thunderstorms that developed over the northern end of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains moved slowly east during the afternoon hours. Outflow boundaries from these two storms advanced eastward toward Interstate 25 and merged with another thunderstorm near Maxwell. The entire area erupted into a large cluster of thunderstorms producing a wet microburst near Maxwell. Several utility poles were toppled in the area. Widespread flooding of low lying areas along Interstate 25 was reported by emergency management however no roads were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail.

The focus for showers and thunderstorms shifted into western New Mexico as shallow low level moisture advanced westward to the Continental Divide on the 23rd. As the afternoon progressed this activity became more a mixture of wet and dry thunderstorms. Strong downburst winds impacted the Gallup airport where a peak wind of 70 mph occurred. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gallup airport reported a 70 mph wind gust from dry thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered storms developed along and east of the central mountains and slowly drifted to the southeast through the evening. One stronger storm dropped heavy rain and strong downburst winds in east central Torrance County, causing a large tree limp to snap and land on a carport. No other damage or reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observer reported strong winds causing a large tree limp to snap and land on carport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The slow-moving upper level storm system responsible for isolated strong thunderstorms over New Mexico on the 14th edged closer to New Mexico on the 15th. A large subtropical moisture tap and associated upper level jet core ahead of this system moved northeast across eastern New Mexico during the late afternoon and evening hours. Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved northeast over the region, some of which became strong to severe. Quarter size hail was reported at Pep. Unfortunately, a woman traveling to Ruidoso from Albuquerque was struck by lightning while on a motorcycle east of Carrizozo. She died several days later in the hospital. Meanwhile, farther north, enough cold air with the approaching upper level storm system allowed snow to accumulate over the northern mountains. Many high elevation areas in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains reported 2 to 12 inches of wet snow, however impacts were limited due to the very high elevation and relatively warm surface temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few quarter size hailstones mixed in with predominately dime size hailstones near Pep.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kalina Jinzo, a 40-year mother of three, was struck by lightning and killed while traveling from Albuquerque to Ruidoso on a motorcycle on the 15th. Her husband was also struck but his injuries were not life threatening. The woman died several days later in a Lubbock hospital around the 22nd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Mexico Department of Transportation closed state road 108 at mile marker 14 due to flooding from the heavy rains.  While flooding may have subsided, the DOT reported that the road will remain closed through memorial day weekend. A CoCoRaHS observer near Pleasant Hill reported 3.25 inches of rain in less than 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front along with northerly flow aloft boosted moisture across northern New Mexico on the 27th. A disturbance within that northerly flow kicked off early morning showers and thunderstorms along and east of the central mountains, moving slowly to the south. Rainfall amounts between half an inch and an inch were common with these storms, with up to 2.2 inches recorded in the Ute Park area. Heavy rains between Eagle Nest and Cimarron caused localized flash flooding that led to 1 fatality at Philmont Scout Ranch and the closure of US Highway 64 for nearly 36 hours. A second round of storms developed by the early afternoon, mainly along and west of the central mountain chain. A couple storms became severe near Jemez Springs and Sandia Park, producing up to half dollar size hail. The severe storm south of Jemez Springs also produced flash flooding that closed state road 485 and forest service road 376 as well as disrupting traffic at low water crossings along state road 4. Major damage was reported to state road 485 from the flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twitter picture showed dime to quarter size hail that fell in the Valles Caldera National Preserve.

The county emergency manager reported significant damage to state road 485. Low water crossings along state road 4 between mile markers 10 and 16 were also flooded, but the road was never closed. New Mexico Department of Transportation representatives also noted that the Rio Guadalupe was out of its banks and possibly into homes.  Further information not confirmed at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms drifted northeast across far northeastern New Mexico. These storms produced several rounds of hail around Raton and numerous funnel clouds from Raton to near Des Moines. One particularly strong storm produced accumulations of dime to penny size hail in Raton. Heavy rainfall was also reported with this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least three storms moved through Raton and each produced light accumulations of pea to dime size hail. The heaviest storm produced penny size hail.

A train of storms over far eastern New Mexico resulted in rainfall amounts anywhere from an inch to over 5 inches, with 5.4 inches reported at the Cannon Air Force Base in less than 24 hours. The 5.4 inches is likely the heaviest 24-hour total rainfall for May in the Clovis area dating back to 1911, with the previous record of 3.45 inches at the Clovis airport. The all-time 24-hour rainfall record for Clovis is 5.26 inches set on September 7, 1957. This heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding within the base and in nearby Clovis through the early morning hours on the 5th. Home evacuations and submerged cars resulted from the flash flooding. No injuries or fatalities resulted from the flooding or hail. Temperatures were cold enough farther north in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains where several inches of snow was reported overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably moisture-rich atmosphere over the Four Corners region combined with a weak upper level low swirling east over the area to produce large hail and flooding. A series of thunderstorms developed immediately west of Farmington during the late morning and intensified rapidly over the area. Golf ball size hail, wall clouds, and flooding were reported. These were some of the strongest storms observed around the Farmington area dating back to the 1950s. Persistent heavy rainfall north of the area for several days in the San Juan Mountains prompted reservoir releases over southwest Colorado. The combination of heavy localized rainfall within the Animas River watershed and releases from Lemon Reservoir forced the Animas River at Farmington to reach minor flood stage for a few hours during the late afternoon and early evening. This was the highest flow observed on the Animas River at Farmington dating back to 2005. Active weather spread east through the remainder of the day with flash flooding around Abiquiu and hail and high winds across the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Animas River rose above bankful during the mid afternoon of the 11th and crested at minor flood stage briefly around 4pm before slowly falling through the overnight. Berg and Animas Parks experienced flooding along the riverwalk and many trails were inundated along the river for several hours. No damage to infrastructure was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough quickly moved east over New Mexico and interacted with abundant low level moisture and instability over the eastern plains to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms. Most of the storms produced pea to dime size hail as they pushed southeast across the area. Dime size hail covered the ground in Abbott up to 2 inches deep. One of the stronger storms in Guadalupe County produced nickel size hail near Newkirk shortly before 5pm. Slight wind damage was reported around the McDonald's in Santa Rosa.

An upper level low was over southern Arizona moving southward, and this feature increased upper level lift over West Texas and southeast New Mexico.  This upper low was pulling moisture northward into the area from the Gulf of Mexico.  These conditions contributed to severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds as well as flash flooding developing across the area.  Thunderstorms increased in coverage overnight as an upper level disturbance moved over the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall developed along the Interstate 40 corridor between Gallup and the Continental Divide. Slow-moving thunderstorms produced 1 to 2 inches of rainfall over already saturated soils in the area. A particularly intense burst of rainfall to the northeast of Gallup, New Mexico resulted in a flash flood within Superman Canyon. Local law enforcement determined that the body of a man found several miles downstream from a crushed pickup truck was the result of this flash flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A truck was found crushed by a large boulder at the base of a 30-ft deep arroyo in Superman Canyon. The body of the man operating the vehicle was discovered 3 miles downstream at White Cliff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another active weather pattern returned to northern and central New Mexico after a prolonged break in the more widespread shower and thunderstorm activity during early July. Heavy rainfall impacted the Jemez and southern Sangre de Cristo Mountains where amounts between 1 and nearly 3 inches were reported from around Los Alamos to Gascon. Another area of heavy rainfall also impacted the east slopes of the Sandia Mountains. Strong winds and very heavy rainfall impacted the eastern plains as this activity rolled eastward off the high terrain. Severe outflow winds around Fort Sumner topped several rail cars off of a BNSF train. A flash flood along Taiban Creek prompted the closure of State Road 294. Several reports of self clouds were received around Clovis as these same storms rolled through the area. A very impressive 4.05 inches of rain fell near Tucumcari, however no reports of flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several foot bridges were washed away within Frijoles Canyon as the creek overflowed it's banks. Water did not make it into the parking lot at the visitor center. Rain gage reports averaged between 1 and 2 inches within the canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southeast low level flow over the region forced abundant moisture all the way westward through the Rio Grande Valley and onto the Continental Divide. Strong thunderstorms developed first over the Albuquerque Metro and east mountain communities before pushing east into the high plains during the late afternoon. Supercell thunderstorms and multicell clusters exploded over the plains and produced large hail and high winds over Lincoln, De Baca, Curry, Roosevelt, and Chaves counties. The largest hail stones up to tennis ball size were observed, as well as wind gusts up to 65 mph. Flash flooding was also reported around Portales where soils were already saturated by weeks of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud spotted just west of the intersection of State Road 13 and U.S. Highway 82.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high east of the state and a surface backdoor cold front drew abundant moisture into northern and central New Mexico through the end of the 28th. The wet environment remained in place on the 29th, leading to widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. Thunderstorms lasted into the morning hours of the 30th, mainly focused over the east central plains. Rainfall amounts were impressive, with numerous reports in excess of an inch. One of the hardest hit locales was Grants, which received between 1.5 and 2.25 inches of rain in little over an hour.  A few streets were flooded, one car was stuck in the flash flood waters, and boats became necessary to travel across other areas. Galisteo Creek below Galisteo also hit flood stage between 510am and 650am on the 30th. Many locales experienced minor flooding impacts, including Santa Rosa where reports of 3 to 4 inches of rain caused El Rito Creek to flow over Power Dam. Sumner Lake received an impressive 4 to 5 inches of rain during the overnight period. This resulted in an astounding rise of nearly 2.5 feet on Sumner Lake. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and Bureau of Reclamation performed reservoir releases up to 300cfs for several days after the event to control water levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Galisteo Creek below the Galisteo Dam reached flood stage of 9 feet for about 2 hours, cresting at 9.18 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The subtropical jet stream that had been positioned over New Mexico for several days was shunted east into the plains on the 6th. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over the area during the late morning before focusing along the central mountain chain during the afternoon hours. This activity merged into a large line segment during the evening that stretched all the way from eastern New Mexico northeastward into the Great Plains. Several ferocious-looking thunderstorms were indicated on Doppler radar, and a report of penny size was received near Maxwell. Otherwise, many areas over eastern New Mexico picked up heavy rainfall, sub-severe hail, and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observer reported quarter size hail fell with heavy rain during the early afternoon.

No business or homes were reported flooded as well as no reported injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dominant high pressure over the Great Basin increased mid level moisture over northern New Mexico within its northerly flow, while a backdoor cold front at the surface increased low level moisture with southerly to southeasterly flow.  The moisture increase plus decent environmental shear and daytime heating led to strong to severe thunderstorms over northern New Mexico.  The strongest storm developed over the Jemez Mountains and moved slowly to the west-southwest, producing quarter sized hail along with heavy rain near Cuba. No other severe or flood reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP observer reported up to quarter size hail fell along with over half an inch of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following heavy rains the previous day or two for many areas of northern and central New Mexico, another round of slow-moving thunderstorms dumped heavy rainfall over central and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening of the 30th. Rainfall accumulations between 0.5 and 1.5 inches in a short period of time were common.  The highest 24-hour rainfall total reported was 2.5 inches in Santa Rosa. The large amounts of rain in a short period of time produced localized flash flooding in north central New Mexico. Flash flooding near Algodones washed out 100 feet of railroad track, suspending the New Mexico Rail Runner train service between Santa Fe and Albuquerque for two days.  Roadways northwest of Madrid also became impassable due to 3 feet of water running over the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall dumped 6.05 inches over Pleasant Hill within 24-hours. This resulted in the closure of State Road 108 due to high water on the roadway. The road was closed for several days in the same area earlier in the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front along with northerly flow aloft boosted moisture across northern New Mexico on the 27th. A disturbance within that northerly flow kicked off early morning showers and thunderstorms along and east of the central mountains, moving slowly to the south. Rainfall amounts between half an inch and an inch were common with these storms, with up to 2.2 inches recorded in the Ute Park area. Heavy rains between Eagle Nest and Cimarron caused localized flash flooding that led to 1 fatality at Philmont Scout Ranch and the closure of US Highway 64 for nearly 36 hours. A second round of storms developed by the early afternoon, mainly along and west of the central mountain chain. A couple storms became severe near Jemez Springs and Sandia Park, producing up to half dollar size hail. The severe storm south of Jemez Springs also produced flash flooding that closed state road 485 and forest service road 376 as well as disrupting traffic at low water crossings along state road 4. Major damage was reported to state road 485 from the flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twitter report indicated half dollar size hail fell along forest service road 604 near Cebollita Spring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably deep upper level low over the Great Basin forced a strong jet streak over New Mexico while the monsoon moisture plume was centered from southwest to northeast over the state. This moist and unstable pattern allowed for the development of several severe thunderstorms with large hail and torrential rainfall. A slow-moving storm near Santa Fe dumped quarter to ping pong ball size hail in Santa Fe. Flash flooding was observed as well. A storm around Isleta Pueblo produced quarter size hail. A mudslide was captured on video near Red River. Highway 38 was closed until the flow subsided and the mud was cleared.

Another round of strong to severe thunderstorms developed over eastern New Mexico on the 7th. A subtropical jet interacting with deep moisture in place over the plains resulted in more heavy rainfall and isolated large hail. The most severe hail occurred around Springer where half dollar size hail was reported. A wall cloud was spotted near Clayton. Areas around Curry County picked up another 1 to 2 inches of rainfall, adding to their already impressive spring rainfall totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter to half dollar size hail near Springer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and strong instability with northwest flow aloft over eastern New Mexico set the stage for yet another round of severe thunderstorms. This was the most significant round of severe weather to impact the area for the month to date. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on the afternoon of 8th and moved southeast into the high plains. A slow-moving severe thunderstorm near Wagon Mound dumped an impressive 1 foot of dime to quarter size hail. Union County was the epicenter for large hail where up to ping pong ball size hail and damaging winds were reported. Several other thunderstorms across east central and northeast New Mexico were also very strong, however no reports of severe weather were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Mexico Department of Transportation reported up to a foot of penny to quarter size hail fell on interstate 25 near Wagon Mound.

Monsoon moisture firmly in place over New Mexico focused another round of very heavy rainfall and severe thunderstorms. Storms with torrential rainfall and strong winds erupted over the state. A storm that developed around Shiprock moved northeast over La Plata and produced flash flooding along U.S. 170. Law enforcement reported that 12 inches of water was flowing over the roadway. A thunderstorm that moved southeast along two colliding outflow boundaries near Edgewood produced a brief tornado. A metal barn for storing hay was tossed a quarter mile and slammed into a house where a woman was injured by flying glass. This same storm also produced quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rotating wall cloud and funnel reported along State Road 41 near McIntosh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably deep upper level low over the Great Basin forced a strong jet streak over New Mexico while the monsoon moisture plume was centered from southwest to northeast over the state. This moist and unstable pattern allowed for the development of several severe thunderstorms with large hail and torrential rainfall. A slow-moving storm near Santa Fe dumped quarter to ping pong ball size hail in Santa Fe. Flash flooding was observed as well. A storm around Isleta Pueblo produced quarter size hail. A mudslide was captured on video near Red River. Highway 38 was closed until the flow subsided and the mud was cleared.

Moderate moisture and instability was located over the Sacramento Mountains. Weak flow aloft kept storm motion very slow and an isolated storm developed near Timberon and produced some localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Official reported that the Sacramento River was over its banks at the Timberon Lodge. Heavy rain was still occurring and streets were flooding in Timberon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and strong instability with northwest flow aloft over eastern New Mexico set the stage for yet another round of severe thunderstorms. This was the most significant round of severe weather to impact the area for the month to date. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on the afternoon of 8th and moved southeast into the high plains. A slow-moving severe thunderstorm near Wagon Mound dumped an impressive 1 foot of dime to quarter size hail. Union County was the epicenter for large hail where up to ping pong ball size hail and damaging winds were reported. Several other thunderstorms across east central and northeast New Mexico were also very strong, however no reports of severe weather were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported dime to ping pong size hail fell in Des Moines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm that developed over the Navajo Reservation during the early morning hours of the 10th produced a brief tornado as it moved northeast near Napi Headquarters southeast of Farmington. Video of the storm showed a funnel cloud hovering over the area for several minutes, followed by a brief touchdown near Indian Service Roads 4002 and 4017 with a large dust swirl for about 2 minutes. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Landspout tornado on the ground for about 2 minutes near Indian Service Roads 4002 and 4017. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over southern Arizona moving southward, and this feature increased upper level lift over West Texas and southeast New Mexico.  This upper low was pulling moisture northward into the area from the Gulf of Mexico.  These conditions contributed to severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds as well as flash flooding developing across the area.  Thunderstorms increased in coverage overnight as an upper level disturbance moved over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Eddy County and produced flash flooding.  Trained weather spotters reported flash flooding of Last Chance Arroyo and up to a foot of water running over Highway 137.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another moist and unstable weather pattern over New Mexico favored more thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall and high winds. A line of thunderstorms that developed over the eastern plains organized into a large cluster over Quay County. A large scale outflow boundary surged northeast out of this area of thunderstorms and slammed the Interstate 40 corridor around San Jon. A couple locations between San Jon and Logan reported outflow winds of 65 mph along with torrential rainfall. No damage was reported with these winds. Several other areas of the state also picked up heavy rainfall and minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 65 mph outflow winds with torrential rainfall in San Jon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably deep upper level low over the Great Basin forced a strong jet streak over New Mexico while the monsoon moisture plume was centered from southwest to northeast over the state. This moist and unstable pattern allowed for the development of several severe thunderstorms with large hail and torrential rainfall. A slow-moving storm near Santa Fe dumped quarter to ping pong ball size hail in Santa Fe. Flash flooding was observed as well. A storm around Isleta Pueblo produced quarter size hail. A mudslide was captured on video near Red River. Highway 38 was closed until the flow subsided and the mud was cleared.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding with estimated 6 inches of flowing water at the corner of Cerrillos and Richards. Gravel washed across the road at the corner of West Alameda and Siler. Landscaping material washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following heavy rains the previous day or two for many areas of northern and central New Mexico, another round of slow-moving thunderstorms dumped heavy rainfall over central and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening of the 30th. Rainfall accumulations between 0.5 and 1.5 inches in a short period of time were common.  The highest 24-hour rainfall total reported was 2.5 inches in Santa Rosa. The large amounts of rain in a short period of time produced localized flash flooding in north central New Mexico. Flash flooding near Algodones washed out 100 feet of railroad track, suspending the New Mexico Rail Runner train service between Santa Fe and Albuquerque for two days.  Roadways northwest of Madrid also became impassable due to 3 feet of water running over the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported that 100 feet of railroad track for the Rail Runner between Albuquerque and Sante Fe was washed out.  The tracks were located on north side of the bridge at the Arroyo Maria Chavez.  Rail Runner service was suspended for the rest of the weekend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following heavy rains the previous day or two for many areas of northern and central New Mexico, another round of slow-moving thunderstorms dumped heavy rainfall over central and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening of the 30th. Rainfall accumulations between 0.5 and 1.5 inches in a short period of time were common.  The highest 24-hour rainfall total reported was 2.5 inches in Santa Rosa. The large amounts of rain in a short period of time produced localized flash flooding in north central New Mexico. Roadways northwest of Madrid also became impassable due to 3 feet of water running over the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos showed water rushing over Red Rock Road near Madrid and making it impassable.  The report indicated that the depth of the water over the road peaked at 3 feet.  It took about an hour for the water to subside enough before safely crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable environment allowed for showers and thunderstorms to develop over much of northern and central New Mexico during the afternoon and evening. Due to the position of the upper level high, storm motion was slow, allowing for thunderstorms to drop heavy rain and small hail over longer periods of time. One half inch to one and a half inches of rainfall was commonly reported, and generally fell within a two hour window. One storm caused flash flooding along State Road 38 near Red River, with the road closing for 2 hours so crews could remove mudslide debris. Another storm caused a 10 foot wall of water to surge through a normally dry gully south of Edgewood. No other flash flooding was reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten feet of water was rushing through a normally dry gulley on the spotters property. No damage was reported as a result of this wall of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daily rounds of showers and thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall elevated the flooding threat over many areas of New Mexico. The persistent wet pattern that began at the end of April and continued through May did not let up during June. Slow-moving thunderstorms around Santa Fe County created minor flooding during the early afternoon around Pojoaque. Another cluster of thunderstorms around Las Vegas prompted flash flood warnings around 5pm. A powerful storm with torrential rainfall slammed the Conchas Dam area and damaged several roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chunks of pavement removed from Big Mesa Road during heavy rainfall. High water was flowing over several portions of the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southeast low level flow over the region forced abundant moisture all the way westward through the Rio Grande Valley and onto the Continental Divide. Strong thunderstorms developed first over the Albuquerque Metro and east mountain communities before pushing east into the high plains during the late afternoon. Supercell thunderstorms and multicell clusters exploded over the plains and produced large hail and high winds over Lincoln, De Baca, Curry, Roosevelt, and Chaves counties. The largest hail stones up to tennis ball size were observed, as well as wind gusts up to 65 mph. Flash flooding was also reported around Portales where soils were already saturated by weeks of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tennis ball size hail in Elida. No damaged reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another moist and unstable weather pattern over New Mexico favored more thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall and high winds. A line of thunderstorms that developed over the eastern plains organized into a large cluster over Quay County. A large scale outflow boundary surged northeast out of this area of thunderstorms and slammed the Interstate 40 corridor around San Jon. A couple locations between San Jon and Logan reported outflow winds of 65 mph along with torrential rainfall. No damage was reported with these winds. Several other areas of the state also picked up heavy rainfall and minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 65 mph outflow winds and over 2 inches of rainfall near Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of upper level high pressure drifted west over Texas, forcing the monsoon moisture plume more into western New Mexico. The combination of afternoon heating and mountainous terrain interacted with all the moisture in place to produce numerous thunderstorms. Several strong to locally severe thunderstorms developed over the Continental Divide and moved slowly northward. One storm in particular along State Road 6 between Los Lunas and Mesita produced minor flooding and large hail. Several other storms in the area also produced flooding and hail however no other reports of severe weather were obtained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail along NM 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture allowed for thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening on the 4th across western and central New Mexico. The combination of heavy rains from slow moving storms and antecedent moisture caused flash flooding to occur in parts of western New Mexico. Heavy rains near Gallup caused State Road 481 to close north of Gallup with flooding and debris on the roadway.  It was also reported that 4 inches of mud plus 8 inches of water got into the juvenile center. Multiple bridges on the east side of Gallup were closed as arroyos were running higher than they have in 5 to 10 years, according to the emergency manager.  Another area of heavy rain flooded a barn along with 2 pastures east of El Morro.  Each flooding case subsided by the 5th.  A 65 mph wind gust also occurred as a result of a wet microburst in south central New Mexico. Torrential rainfall likely resulted in localized flash flooding in other locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused State Road 491 north of Gallup to close due to flooding and debris. County Emergency Manager reported flash flooding caused 4 inches of mud and 8 inches of water to enter a juvenile center on the east side of town. He also noted that a few bridges were temporarily closed due to flood waters, along with numerous county roads suffering damage. Exact location and extent of damage was unknown. Allen Joe Begay of White Cone, Arizona was swept away in flood waters while in the Rio Puerco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of Western Grant County remained in Severe(D2) drought during the month of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The western portion of the zone remained in Severe(D2) drought through the entire month of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressively moist and unstable atmosphere in place over New Mexico on the 6th produced widespread heavy rainfall and even a few severe thunderstorms. Slow-moving thunderstorms produced wet microburst winds, hail, and intense lightning strikes. A storm immediately east of Clovis produced an estimated 75 mph wind gust, toppling a grain elevator, damaging 2 others and a nearby vehicle. One injury was reported. Another storm along State Road 53 south of Grants dumped 6 inches of hail, resulting in a major traffic accident with injuries. Another storm to the southeast of Cuervo produced an estimated 80 mph wind gust, producing extensive damage on a private ranch. A house was struck by lightning in Roosevelt County and caught fire. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches resulted in many cases of minor flooding, however no flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large wooden doors were blown off a barn. Several trees had severe damage. A plastic roof on a greenhouse was blown off. A metal tail of a windmill was wrapped around the wheel base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture firmly in place over New Mexico focused another round of very heavy rainfall and severe thunderstorms. Storms with torrential rainfall and strong winds erupted over the state. A storm that developed around Shiprock moved northeast over La Plata and produced flash flooding along U.S. 170. Law enforcement reported that 12 inches of water was flowing over the roadway. A thunderstorm that moved southeast along two colliding outflow boundaries near Edgewood produced a brief tornado. A metal barn for storing hay was tossed a quarter mile and slammed into a house where a woman was injured by flying glass. This same storm also produced quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter size hail in Edgewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressively moist and unstable atmosphere in place over New Mexico on the 6th produced widespread heavy rainfall and even a few severe thunderstorms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet microburst winds toppled a metal grain silo onto a nearby truck, resulting in 1 injury. Two other silos were also severely damaged. Numerous tree branches were broken at a nearby home. A spotter reported storm total rainfall of 3.25 inches from these storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably moisture-rich atmosphere over the Four Corners region combined with a weak upper level low swirling east over the area to produce large hail and flooding. A series of thunderstorms developed immediately west of Farmington during the late morning and intensified rapidly over the area. Golf ball size hail, wall clouds, and flooding were reported. These were some of the strongest storms observed around the Farmington area dating back to the 1950s. Persistent heavy rainfall north of the area for several days in the San Juan Mountains prompted reservoir releases over southwest Colorado. The combination of heavy localized rainfall within the Animas River watershed and releases from Lemon Reservoir forced the Animas River at Farmington to reach minor flood stage for a few hours during the late afternoon and early evening. This was the highest flow observed on the Animas River at Farmington dating back to 2005. Active weather spread east through the remainder of the day with flash flooding around Abiquiu and hail and high winds across the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail in Farmington.

Several strong to locally severe thunderstorms developed over the Continental Divide and moved slowly northward. One storm in particular along State Road 6 between Los Lunas and Mesita produced minor flooding and large hail. Several other storms in the area also produced flooding and hail however no other reports of severe weather were obtained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to roads and guard rails along canyons west of Interstate 25, particularly within Nogal Canyon.
