EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a mudslide along NM 38, causing it to close for 2 hours.  The rain also caused a rockslide to close all but one lane of County Road 578.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture firmly in place over New Mexico focused another round of very heavy rainfall and severe thunderstorms. Storms with torrential rainfall and strong winds erupted over the state. A storm that developed around Shiprock moved northeast over La Plata and produced flash flooding along U.S. 170. Law enforcement reported that 12 inches of water was flowing over the roadway. A thunderstorm that moved southeast along two colliding outflow boundaries near Edgewood produced a brief tornado. A metal barn for storing hay was tossed a quarter mile and slammed into a house where a woman was injured by flying glass. This same storm also produced quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 30' x 20' metal hay barn was tossed a quarter mile and crashed into a nearby home. Flying glass injured a woman in the home. Several folks reported a brief tornado on the ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flood wave moved down Yeso Rito arroyo in less than 30 minutes, resulting in significant damage at Ghost Ranch. At least three older structures and a ropes course were completely destroyed. One newer studio structure was seriously damaged. Trees were snapped in half and mud filled several other structures. Damage has been estimated between 500,000 and 1 million dollars. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A foot of water flowed over U.S. 170 between mile markers 15 and 18. NMDOT had to clean mud, rocks, and debris off the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail at San Juan College. Second hand report noted some sky lights were damaged in a couple buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable atmosphere in place along with weak steering flow set the stage for numerous thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall. One storm in particular to the west of Ruidoso near Bonito Lake produced a significant localized flash flood near Orchard Campground and Marthas Canyon. Several points on the road were washed over with flood waters, mud, rocks, and debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant wash out with debris near Orchard Campground west of Bonito Lake. A couple places along highway 107 were impassable due to flowing water and debris. Marthas Canyon was the hardest hit.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused a barn and two pastures to be flooded. One lane of a two-lane gravel road was also washed out with many other roads seeing 3 feet deep ravines created by the heavy rain. Numerous roads around the Zuni Mountains were washed out and residents were trapped in their homes for several days until county officials could repair the roads. Phone services were also lost.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo from emergency manager showed ping pong ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate amounts of moisture were lingering over Southwest New Mexico with an upper level disturbance moving through westerly flow aloft. A severe thunderstorm developed over northwest Luna County with winds over 60 mph reported which caused some damage near the Grant/Luna County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Silver City AWOS recorded a peak gust of 61 mph along with rain totaling 2.96 inches over a 2 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture over the Gila was able to bring the western areas out of Severe(D2) drought and into Moderate(D1) conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The far western areas of Grant County were dropped from Severe(D2) to Moderate(D1) drought conditions in the middle of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elongated upper trough extended from the southern plains through far west Texas into Mexico. Low level moisture was pushed to about the Rio Grande Valley of New Mexico to help produce locally heavy rain. There was also ample wind shear to support severe hail.
A mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow fell on southeast New Mexico and west Texas as an arctic airmass resided over the region, and ahead of a slow moving upper trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six and one half inches of snow reported in Artesia, on top of additional ice accumulation from earlier in the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dryline was located just east of the Rio Grande Valley with northeast flow aloft which allowed some storms to develop off the higher terrain of Dona Ana County and move southwest into Las Cruces. Some wind damage was reported on the NMSU campus from a dying thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three large trees were blown down on the NMSU campus at the married housing units.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper high was located over Southern Arizona with north to northeast flow aloft across Southern New Mexico. Low level flow was out of the east providing good low level moisture and sufficient shear for severe thunderstorms to develop. Wind damage was reported around Silver City and Tularosa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated wind gusts to 60 mph. Several branches were down on College Avenue between NM-90 and the WNMU Campus, including College and Pope.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was over Southern California with increasing southwest flow aloft over the region, including an 80kt jet entering Southern New Mexico. Good moisture was in place over the area with moderate instability and wind shear. A few strong storms developed over Sierra County and produced severe hail and some flash flooding.

An upper low was over Southern California with increasing southwest flow aloft over the region, including an 80kt jet entering Southern New Mexico. Good moisture was in place over the area with moderate instability and wind shear. A few strong storms developed over Sierra County and produced severe hail and some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 52 near Winston was closed due to flooding at the low water crossings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure was over the Southern Plains, and a surface lee trough was located across eastern New Mexico southward into the eastern slopes of the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains.  Hot and moist air leading to good instability was present across southeast New Mexico.  These conditions allowed storms to develop in the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains then move into the adjacent plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm moved across Lea County and produced wind damage in and around Hobbs.  A local storm spotter reported power lines down and a mobile home blown over on Jones Road.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure was over the Southern Plains, and a surface lee trough was located across eastern New Mexico southward into the eastern slopes of the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains.  Hot and moist air leading to good instability was present across southeast New Mexico.  These conditions allowed storms to develop in the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains then move into the adjacent plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm moved across Lea County and produced wind damage northwest of Hobbs.  A local storm spotter reported building damage and power lines down near the intersection of Bensing and Lawrence Roads.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms developed over southeastern New Mexico during the late afternoon of the 3rd. This area of storms produced gusty winds and heavy rainfall. One particularly heavy storm south of Roswell toppled an 18-wheeler on U.S. Highway 285. The driver was injured in the crash and transported to a local hospital. A more potent cluster of severe thunderstorms developed over far northeast New Mexico during the early evening hours and delivered large hail and high winds. Quarter size hail and 58 mph winds were reported as the cluster moved slowly east from Grenville to Clayton and Seneca. Very heavy rain also fell in the area however no flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail near Grenville. Light accumulations were noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms developed over southeastern New Mexico during the late afternoon of the 3rd. This area of storms produced gusty winds and heavy rainfall. One particularly heavy storm south of Roswell toppled an 18-wheeler on U.S. Highway 285. The driver was injured in the crash and transported to a local hospital. A more potent cluster of severe thunderstorms developed over far northeast New Mexico during the early evening hours and delivered large hail and high winds. Quarter size hail and 58 mph winds were reported as the cluster moved slowly east from Grenville to Clayton and Seneca. Very heavy rain also fell in the area however no flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported overturned 18-wheeler along U.S. Highway 285 from severe thunderstorm winds. The driver was injured in the crash and transported to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was moving into the Southern Great Basin with west-southwest flow aloft across the Borderland. Deep monsoon moisture was in place with dew points into the upper 50s to lower 60s. Good wind shear and instability provided the necessary ingredients for severe hail and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was located over Central Texas with deep south to southeast flow across the Rio Grande Valley bringing deep moisture into the region.  A surface trough was located near the Rio Grande providing some low level lift. Heavy rain occurred in and around Alamogordo with over 3 inches reported in the northeast portion of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of 2 to over 3 inches were reported around Alamogordo with multiple road closures put into place. Water was across several roads 6 to 8 inches deep. Winds of up to 50 mph also occurred with the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was over the Great Basin and an upper trough was over the Midwest.  There were upslope winds into the higher terrain of southeast New Mexico and West Texas during the afternoon and residual low level boundaries across the area which helped with low level lift and convergence.  There was a jet over the region and an upper level disturbance over the Texas panhandle which helped to increase upper lift.  These conditions, combined with decent moisture across the area, allowed for thunderstorms with strong winds and hail to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Eddy County and produced a 61 mph wind gust near Carlsbad as recorded by the Carlsbad ASOS station.

A weak low pressure system slowly drifted east into the Great Plains with a trailing upper level disturbance rotating around the backside of the upper low.  As the disturbance moved over the region with high moisture in place, it brought thunderstorms with high winds to southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Eddy County and produced a 60 mph wind gust two miles north of Seven Rivers.

An upper high was located over Central Texas and a weak disturbance in southerly flow was moving into New Mexico.  Marginal instability allowed a strong thunderstorm with dime size hail to develop over Southern Dona Ana County.

Upper high was centered over the Texas Panhandle and southeast flow was bringing moderate moisture into the region. An isolated slow moving storm dropped over 3 inches of rain near La Luz and the water drained down the west slopes of the Sacramento Mountains and caused water to cross the road west of Highway 54.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was crossing Riata Road west of U.S. 54. Rain had fallen several hours earlier up near La Luz and flowed down toward Alamogordo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly dry atmosphere was in place with upper high moving into Northeast Texas. Deep southerly flow was in place across the region and a weak disturbance helped isolated thunderstorms to develop and a brief landspout tornado was reported west of Ruidoso.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video of a tornado west of Ruidoso received a lot of social media attention.  The tornado was located in front of Sierra Blanca Peak around 9000 ft in elevation. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elongated upper trough extended from the southern plains through far west Texas into Mexico. Low level moisture was pushed to about the Rio Grande Valley of New Mexico to help produce locally heavy rain. There was also ample wind shear to support severe hail.

A strong upper high was located over Southern Arizona with north to northeast flow aloft across Southern New Mexico. Low level flow was out of the east providing good low level moisture and sufficient shear for severe thunderstorms to develop. Wind damage was reported around Silver City and Tularosa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds blew a fence down in Tularosa. An inch of rain also fell in about 20 minutes with the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high continued to sit over Southern Arizona with northerly flow across Southern New Mexico. Moderate moisture remained in place across the region with dew points in the lower 50s and a surface trough across the Rio Grande Valley. A severe thunderstorm developed over the southern portion of Elephant Butte Lake and produced estimated wind gusts over 70 mph and did significant damage at the marina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm survey revealed numerous boats damaged at Marina Del Sur on the south end of Elephant Butte Lake. A couple of metal roofs covering the boats were torn off and/or collapsed and concrete slabs anchoring down the structures were pulled out of the water. An eyewitness described a downburst lasting 15 minutes with torrential rainfall and zero visibility. Property damage was in excess of $1 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate amounts of moisture were lingering over Southwest New Mexico with an upper level disturbance moving through westerly flow aloft. A severe thunderstorm developed over northwest Luna County with winds over 60 mph reported which caused some damage near the Grant/Luna County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retractable roof became airborne and landed on the roof of a trailer and caused it to become punched-in. An anemometer near the damaged site recorded a peak wind of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February 2015 precipitation was a tale of two halves.  The first 10 days of the month were especially dry areawide.  Record warmth during the second week took a major toll on the existing and rather meager mountain snowpack.  Yet another impressive stretch of record or near record warmth on the 18th through 20th gave way to much colder temperatures and far more active and snowy pattern to close out the month.  In fact, Albuquerque and portions of central New Mexico experienced record-breaking snow from late on the 25th through the 28th.  Snowpack gains in the central and northern mountains were significant.  Only slight improvements in he overall drought status were noted, however, with approximately one-quarter of the state classified in severe drought (D2) or worse.  That was down nearly 5 percent from the previous month.  Drought in the Four Corners area from Gallup to Shiprock remained the most severe.

A closed upper level low that developed southwest of San Diego California moved into central Arizona early on the 30th before shifting south into Mexico on the 31st. This system drew significant subtropical moisture northward into the area, with nearly record high precipitable water values that were around 3 standard deviations above the mean.  Mid level warm air advection prevented a more widespread snow event, limiting major snowfall accumulations to mainly areas above 7500 feet. Even then, a significantly deep warm layer on the 31st created a mix of rain, snow, and sleet for locations as high up as 10000 feet. Fifteen inches of snow was reported in the Jemez Mountains with nearly 2 inches of rain falling in the Glenwood area. High flows, mud, rocks, and debris were reported along the access road to the Catwalk in Catron County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS and public reports indicated between 2 and 8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storm systems tracked slowly through New Mexico from the 12th to the 14th and produced a long duration period of winter weather. The first storm system moved eastward out of Arizona and through New Mexico central and southern New Mexico. Temperatures with the first system hovered close to freezing so most of the significant impacts were experienced primarily in the higher terrain. The second system dove southeast out of the Pacific Northwest and become quasi-stationary over Arizona before weakening as it pushed through New Mexico. This second storm produced the most widespread snowfall and greatest impacts across central and western New Mexico. The most significant accumulations occurred along the Continental Divide and the northwest mountains where 6 to 12 inches were reported. Several schools and businesses were closed or operated on 2-hour delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS, trained spotters, and law enforcement reported 6 to 7 inches of snowfall, primarily along Interstate 40 between Grants, Acoma Pueblo, and Laguna Pueblo. Significant impacts to travel were reported along Interstate 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: New Mexico benefited from several impactful winter storms during January 2015. Precipitation was mostly above normal except for the northwest and far west. One of the most significant winter storms of the season brought widespread, major snowfall to many areas on the 21st and 22nd. Another impressive storm dumped widespread rain and snow from late on the 29th into the 31st.  There was very little change in the overall drought status across New Mexico with about one-third of the state in severe drought (D2) or worse, mostly focused across the west and northeast.  Extreme drought conditions continued in the Four Corners Region from Gallup north to Shiprock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two upper level disturbances phased over southwestern Arizona and developed into a closed low on the 21st, moving in unison to the south and east into west central Texas by the 23rd.  The system was able to produce heavy snow and blowing snow across much of north central and eastern New Mexico by drawing deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and combining with a backdoor cold front.  The highest snowfall totals were reported along the east slopes of the Sandia and northern Sacramento mountains, where anywhere from 10 inches to 2 feet fell. Numerous schools and businesses were closed as well as U.S. Highway 82 in southwest Chaves County, state road 72 east of Raton, and state road 53 near El Morro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February 2015 precipitation was a tale of two halves.  The first 10 days of the month were especially dry areawide.  Record warmth during the second week took a major toll on the existing and rather meager mountain snowpack.  Yet another impressive stretch of record or near record warmth on the 18th through 20th gave way to much colder temperatures and far more active and snowy pattern to close out the month.  In fact, Albuquerque and portions of central New Mexico experienced record-breaking snow from late on the 25th through the 28th.  Snowpack gains in the central and northern mountains were significant.  Only slight improvements in he overall drought status were noted, however, with approximately one-quarter of the state classified in severe drought (D2) or worse.  That was down nearly 5 percent from the previous month.  Drought in the Four Corners area from Gallup to Shiprock remained the most severe.

New Mexico benefited from several impactful winter storms during January 2015. Precipitation was mostly above normal except for the northwest and far west. One of the most significant winter storms of the season brought widespread, major snowfall to many areas on the 21st and 22nd. Another impressive storm dumped widespread rain and snow from late on the 29th into the 31st.  There was very little change in the overall drought status across New Mexico with about one-third of the state in severe drought (D2) or worse, mostly focused across the west and northeast.  Extreme drought conditions continued in the Four Corners Region from Gallup north to Shiprock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low moved through the Four Corners area and brought with it some Pacific moisture. East winds at the surface provided the cold air needed to produce the snow.  The hardest hit areas were in the Gila Region where almost a foot of snow was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Mule Creek reported 10 inches of snow and a Cooperative Observer reported 7 inches in Gila Hot Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A positively tilted upper trough was dropping through the Four Corners region. At the same time, a strong surface high extending from the Great Basin into the central high plains was bringing in cold surface temperatures from the northeast. These features combined to create a band of heavy snow from eastern Luna County into northern Otero County where up to 9 inches of snow was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative Observer in Sacramento reported 9 inches of snowfall. A CoCoRaHS Observer 5 miles northeast of Cloudcroft reported 7.4 inches of snow with the town of Cloudcroft receiving 7.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low located along the Arizona New Mexico border produced snow showers across much of western and central New Mexico late on the 31st through the day on the 1st.  Snow showers were mainly focused along a weak Pacific cold front that quickly moved northeast. Snowfall amounts were generally light, ranging from 1 to 3 inches, but up to 5 inches was reported west of Gallup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters indicated between 2 to 5 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system began to close off over southern New Mexico on the 2nd.  A significant moisture tap ahead of the low produced an initial wave of freezing sleet and snow across the eastern plains during the 2nd, while snow showers around the wrapped low fell across the southwest mountains. As the system progressed east late on the 2nd and into the 3rd, wrap-around moisture produced significant snowfall across much of south central and eastern New Mexico.  Up to 16 inches of snow accumulated across the east central plains, causing major impacts to travel along Interstate 40 and various state and county roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observers and CoCoRaHS reports indicated between 2 to 6 inches of snow.

In fact, Albuquerque and portions of central New Mexico experienced record-breaking snow from late on the 25th through the 28th.  Snowpack gains in the central and northern mountains were significant.  Only slight improvements in he overall drought status were noted, however, with approximately one-quarter of the state classified in severe drought (D2) or worse.  That was down nearly 5 percent from the previous month.  Drought in the Four Corners area from Gallup to Shiprock remained the most severe.

Two upper level disturbances phased over southwestern Arizona and developed into a closed low on the 21st, moving in unison to the south and east into west central Texas by the 23rd.  The system was able to produce heavy snow and blowing snow across much of north central and eastern New Mexico by drawing deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and combining with a backdoor cold front.  The highest snowfall totals were reported along the east slopes of the Sandia and northern Sacramento mountains, where anywhere from 10 inches to 2 feet fell. Numerous schools and businesses were closed as well as U.S. Highway 82 in southwest Chaves County, state road 72 east of Raton, and state road 53 near El Morro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports indicate between 2 and 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system began to close off over southern New Mexico on the 2nd.  A significant moisture tap ahead of the low produced an initial wave of freezing sleet and snow across the eastern plains during the 2nd, while snow showers around the wrapped low fell across the southwest mountains. As the system progressed east late on the 2nd and into the 3rd, wrap-around moisture produced significant snowfall across much of south central and eastern New Mexico.  Up to 16 inches of snow accumulated across the east central plains, causing major impacts to travel along Interstate 40 and various state and county roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and CoCoRaHS reports indicated 5 to 8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low that developed southwest of San Diego California moved into central Arizona early on the 30th before shifting south into Mexico on the 31st. This system drew significant subtropical moisture northward into the area, with nearly record high precipitable water values that were around 3 standard deviations above the mean.  Mid level warm air advection prevented a more widespread snow event, limiting major snowfall accumulations to mainly areas above 7500 feet. Even then, a significantly deep warm layer on the 31st created a mix of rain, snow, and sleet for locations as high up as 10000 feet. Fifteen inches of snow was reported in the Jemez Mountains with nearly 2 inches of rain falling in the Glenwood area. High flows, mud, rocks, and debris were reported along the access road to the Catwalk in Catron County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports indicated between 4 to 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storm systems tracked slowly through New Mexico from the 12th to the 14th and produced a long duration period of winter weather. The first storm system moved eastward out of Arizona and through New Mexico central and southern New Mexico. Temperatures with the first system hovered close to freezing so most of the significant impacts were experienced primarily in the higher terrain. The second system dove southeast out of the Pacific Northwest and become quasi-stationary over Arizona before weakening as it pushed through New Mexico. This second storm produced the most widespread snowfall and greatest impacts across central and western New Mexico. The most significant accumulations occurred along the Continental Divide and the northwest mountains where 6 to 12 inches were reported. Several schools and businesses were closed or operated on 2-hour delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Navajo Whiskey SNOTEL reported 12 inches of snowfall. The bulk of this accumulation occurred on the 13th.

This second storm produced the most widespread snowfall and greatest impacts across central and western New Mexico. The most significant accumulations occurred along the Continental Divide and the northwest mountains where 6 to 12 inches were reported. Several schools and businesses were closed or operated on 2-hour delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and trained spotter reported between 4 and 8 inches of snowfall, primarily along the Continental Divide. Significant impacts to travel were reported along U.S. Highway 550. The area around Cuba and Lindrith received the heaviest snowfall accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid-level jet streak moved over central New Mexico on the 8th and combined with a deep surface trough to produce strong southwest winds over portions of central New Mexico.  Wind gusts between 40 and 50 mph were common across the state with the strongest winds mainly focused over south central New Mexico.  Various sites from Eastern Socorro County through Eastern Lincoln County recorded wind gusts between 61 and 67 mph.  A brief gust of 58 mph was also recorded along the southwest slopes of the Sangre De Cristo Mountains.  No damage was reported for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry and warm start to the month of February ended with an extremely beneficial pattern change that delivered epic snowfall amounts to the northern high terrain of New Mexico. The big change arrived beginning on the 22nd as a powerful blast of arctic air moved south and west across the area and plunged New Mexico into winter once again. Meanwhile, a series of upper level low pressure systems crossed from southern California into Arizona and pumped abundant moisture over New Mexico. The heaviest snowfall accumulations occurred over the northern high terrain where 1 to 2 feet of new snow was reported. A strong surface pressure gradient in place over the area also produced strong gap winds in the Rio Grande Valley. Widespread difficult to severe driving conditions were reported along with several road closures over portions of New Mexico. This was the first in a series of significant winter storms that impacted the area through early March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highway 70 was closed for several hours beginning around 530am on the 23rd from Roswell to Hondo and Ruidoso due to light snow, freezing fog and black ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This heavy snow event followed quickly on the heels of the initial heavy snow event from the 22nd to the 24th. Several upper level disturbances embedded in a deep fetch of abundant subtropical moisture continued shifting northeastward over New Mexico. This moisture and instability interacted with a record cold airmass entrenched over the state. Numerous locations reported record low maximum temperatures. Snow was measured in feet over the northern high terrain where record-breaking amounts were reported. This was the best period of snow in years for many resort locations across the northern mountains. Snowpack received a huge boost as many SNOTELs overcame huge deficits to end the month near normal to even above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP reports averaged between 6 and 9 inches storm total snow during this extended winter storm. Frigid temperatures and gusty winds created deeper drifts and hazardous travel conditions.

This moisture and instability interacted with a record cold airmass entrenched over the state. Numerous locations reported record low maximum temperatures. Snow was measured in feet over the northern high terrain where record-breaking amounts were reported. This was the best period of snow in years for many resort locations across the northern mountains. Snowpack received a huge boost as many SNOTELs overcame huge deficits to end the month near normal to even above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP, CoCoRaHS, SNOTEL, and public reports averaged between 13 and 33 inches storm total snow during this extended winter storm. The 33 inches at Wolf Canyon was the 5th heaviest 4-day snowfall event ever reported at the site dating back to 1912. The 16 inches on the 28th also set a new record for the date.

The 33 inches for the 2-day period ending on the 1st was the 7th heaviest snowfall event dating back to 1893. In El Vado, the 12 inches on the 28th and the 10 inches on the 1st were a new records for the date, respectively. The 25 inches from the 27th through the 1st in El Vado set a new record for 3-day accumulations dating back to 1906.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent high pressure over New Mexico caused a prolonged period of near record to record high temperatures for central and eastern areas.  Very warm temperatures on the 9th combined with low humidity values and windy conditions to create critical fire weather conditions for portions of east central New Mexico.  A grass fire started around 145pm 60 miles west of Roswell, or near Hondo.  Forestry officials were able to contain the 10 acre fire about an hour later, but not before an outbuilding was destroyed.  No other injuries or property damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NM State Forestry reported 10 acre fire through Twitter.  KOAT later confirmed fire and burning of one building.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extraordinary number of reports were received during this prolonged winter event. This was the most significant event to impact the Albuquerque metro area since 2006. The Albuquerque International Sunport broke daily snowfall records on both the 27th and the 28th. It was the 9th heaviest 2-day snowfall event on record dating back to 1931. The first impulse of snow early on the 27th produced a couple inches of snow followed by a lull during the afternoon. The most intense snowfall impacted the area in a very heavy band from the evening of the 27th to sunrise on the 28th. Interstate 40 west of Albuquerque closed late on the 26th and the on-ramp from Coors Road onto Interstate 40 EB closed during the morning on the 27th due to snowl and icy conditions. This snow also caused Rio Rancho public schools to close on the 27th.  The interchange from Interstate 25 NB onto Interstate 40 WB closed late on the 27th into the 28th due to heavy snow and ice on the overpass. Both Albuquerque and Rio Rancho public schools closed on the 28th.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved out of the central Rockies late on the 10th and into boot heel of New Mexico late in the evening on the 11th. A potent backdoor cold front accompanied this system, providing enough cold air and upslope flow for significant snow to fall along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. Eight to 12 inches fell near the peaks of the mountains with lower amounts in nearby valleys. Winds behind the backdoor cold front were strongest through the White Sands Missile Range with gusts up to 61 mph.  No damage was reported due to the wind or snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOTEL reports indicated between 4 to 10 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving upper level disturbance combined with a backdoor cold front to bring snow showers across northern and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon of the 26th through the morning of the 27th. Bands of heavy snow showers fell across the northeast highlands, with lighter bands producing significant snow gradients in snowfall across the rest of the eastern plains. Snowfall totals were generally between 2 to 4 inches with reports up to 6 inches between Springer and Maxwell. No accidents or road closures were reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter and public reports indicated between 2 to 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system approaching from the Great Basin combined with a lee side trough over eastern New Mexico to produce breezy to windy conditions over the area. These showers allowed winds to mix down to the surface more efficiently, resulting in a few strong wind gust reports. The strongest winds were reported from White Sands to Clines Corners and Tucumcari.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry and warm start to the month of February ended with an extremely beneficial pattern change that delivered epic snowfall amounts to the northern high terrain of New Mexico. The big change arrived beginning on the 22nd as a powerful blast of arctic air moved south and west across the area and plunged New Mexico into winter once again. Meanwhile, a series of upper level low pressure systems crossed from southern California into Arizona and pumped abundant moisture over New Mexico. The heaviest snowfall accumulations occurred over the northern high terrain where 1 to 2 feet of new snow was reported. A strong surface pressure gradient in place over the area also produced strong gap winds in the Rio Grande Valley. Widespread difficult to severe driving conditions were reported along with several road closures over portions of New Mexico. This was the first in a series of significant winter storms that impacted the area through early March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS, public, and SNOTEL sites reported between 3 and 10 inches of new snow over the area. This was the first big event in nearly one month for the west slopes.  The 8 inches recorded on the 22nd and the 6 inches recorded on the 23rd in Cerro set new records for each calendar date, respectively. It was the heaviest 2-day total in Cerro since 2008.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was moving through Southern California with southwest flow aloft across the region.  A surface low and associated cold front moved through Southern New Mexico. Dew points were well into the 50s and southeast surface winds provided sufficient wind shear for severe thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The slow-moving upper level storm system responsible for wild spring weather over New Mexico between the 14th and 15th delivered one more round of wild weather from the 16th to early on the 17th. The combination of low level moisture, mid level drying, and colder temperatures aloft provided instability for more showers and thunderstorms over eastern New Mexico and snow over the northern mountains. Relatively benign thunderstorms that impacted the far eastern plains produced wind gusts to 60mph in several areas. Farther north, more accumulating snow was reported in the high terrain. A few more inches of snow were reported however impacts again were limited due to warm surface temperatures. Storm total amounts on the order of 12 to 17 inches were reported around Taos Ski Valley over the 2-day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site near Dora reported a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent backdoor cold front plowed through the eastern plains through the day of the 25th, reaching the central mountains by the evening hours.  The strongest gradient developed through the mountain gaps near Carrizozo within the White Sands Missile Range, resulting in a peak gust of 66 mph.  No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site in the White Sands Missile Range measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak ridging over New Mexico created a period of above normal temperatures and low humidities. A grass fire started around noon 10 miles south of Moriarty, or near McIntosh. Firefighters were able to contain the 35 acre fire about a few hours later, but not before two sheds were destroyed. No other injuries or property damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KOAT-TV reported a 35 acre fire through Twitter, later confirmed fire burning of two sheds.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET/RED RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The slow-moving upper level storm system responsible for wild spring weather over New Mexico between the 14th and 15th delivered one more round of wild weather from the 16th to early on the 17th. The combination of low level moisture, mid level drying, and colder temperatures aloft provided instability for more showers and thunderstorms over eastern New Mexico and snow over the northern mountains. Relatively benign thunderstorms that impacted the far eastern plains produced wind gusts to 60mph in several areas. Farther north, more accumulating snow was reported in the high terrain. A few more inches of snow were reported however impacts again were limited due to warm surface temperatures. Storm total amounts on the order of 12 to 17 inches were reported around Taos Ski Valley over the 2-day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several more inches of wet snow in the very high elevations of the northern Sangre de Cristo Mountains tallied between 12 and 17 inches for the 2-day period around Taos Ski Valley. Impacts were limited due to the high elevation and relatively warm surface temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry, windy and warm conditions dominated the first 10 days of April 2015. An impressive rain event then unfolded across the southeast third of the state on the 12th and 13th. Numerous one to two inch rainfall amounts were reported across Lincoln, Chaves and Roosevelt counties. A minor to moderate snow event helped to bring some precipitation to the north on the 18th. A welcomed, widespread rain and high northern mountain snow event impacted the state from the 26th into the 28th. The month ended mostly dry and warm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: KTCS ASOS reported a peak wind of 67 mph from a downdraft associated with weak convective showers in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave ejected out of Mexico on the nose of a subtropical jet. As the upper level disturbance moved over the area, there was ample moisture in place across West Texas. Good instability and shear east of the dryline lead to organized severe storms producing large hail across southeast New Mexico.

May brought some above normal precipitation to the region, but the far western portions of Grant County remained in severe drought through the month of May.

Upper level low pressure area centered near Baja moved toward West Texas and then slowly across the Permian Basin and the Texas Panhandle, which provided enough lift over the area to produce showers and thunderstorms.
A slightly below average month for precipitation in the Gila Region kept a small portion of Grant County in Severe(D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The far western portion of the zone remained in Severe(D2) drought for the month of April. The Gila Hot Springs Cooperative Observer reported 0.30 inches of precipitation which was 0.13 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level storm system that slowly moved east from southern California produced scattered showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall over New Mexico. An area of strong thunderstorms that moved toward the northeast over Cibola County during the mid to late afternoon hours on the 4th produced significant flash flooding around the Grants area. Rainfall reports were not incredibly impressive. However, the heaviest amounts fell in an area susceptible to flash flooding. The abundant amount of hail that also occurred with the storm made conditions much worse as culverts became clogged with ice, mud, rocks, and debris. The northwest slope of Mount Taylor to near Encinal and Bibo were particularly hard hit. Several bridges were damaged from flooding along with numerous roads, culverts, and jersey barriers. The most significant damage impacted the area around the George Hannish Bridge and county roads 19A and 19D.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three bridges were damaged from flood waters northeast of Grants. A dozen jersey barriers were washed 100 yards downstream at low water crossings along Lobo Canyon. The most significant damage occurred to the George Hannish Bridge. Bridges along county roads 19A and 19D were also impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The deep low pressure system that brought severe weather to New Mexico on the 19th continued churning slowly eastward toward the state on the 20th. A widespread burst of showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall and severe weather surged across much of the area along and south of Interstate 40. Numerous reports of large hail and flooding were received. The most significant flooding occurred south of Roswell along Highway 2 where roads were closed and cars were stranded in high water. Large hail reports were common within Catron, Roosevelt, Chaves, and Guadalupe counties. Several locations reported quarter to even golf ball size hail.

An upper level low pressure system that slowly moved southward through California provided deep southerly flow over New Mexico. Subtropical moisture surged northward ahead of this system in several waves over New Mexico. The first wave of moisture and instability moved over the area early on October 19th and created severe thunderstorms from the predawn hours through the late morning. A couple inches of severe hail moved over Socorro and impacted travel along Interstate 25. Storms continued to develop through the morning and impacted areas of Valencia and Chaves counties with mainly nickel to quarter sized hail. Another round of thunderstorms with large hail impacted the region later in the day around Los Alamos, Espanola, and parts of the eastern plains.

A fast-moving cold frontal boundary shifting south and west across eastern New Mexico slammed through gaps in the central mountains around 8pm on October 15th. The combination of a strong pressure gradient at the surface and deep easterly flow from the surface to mountain top level allowed for a partial spill over event on the east side of the Albuquerque metro area. Peak winds topped out near 65 mph from the foothills to the Sunport. Several trees, power lines, business signs, and fences were downed across the area. Blowing dust and sand reduced visibilities to near 5 miles. Dangerous crosswinds impacted Interstate 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust to 66 mph at 1149pm MDT. Sustained winds peaked at 52 mph. Numerous trees and powerlines down across the east side. Unofficial site within Embudo Canyon peaked at 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The low pressure system responsible for an extended stretch of active weather across New Mexico finally ejected through the state on the 21st. The last bout delivered an impressive batch of widespread heavy rainfall and scattered strong to severe thunderstorms. Large hail was common with severe storms across the eastern plains, particularly around De Baca, Roosevelt, and Curry counties where golf ball to tennis ball size hail was common. Heavy rainfall amounts between 2 and 4 inches were common as well across the area from Roosevelt to Curry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Mexico State road 246 closed between mile markers 59 and 60 due to flooding over the roadway.

Large hail reports were common within Catron, Roosevelt, Chaves, and Guadalupe counties. Several locations reported quarter to even golf ball size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The low pressure system responsible for an extended stretch of active weather across New Mexico finally ejected through the state on the 21st. The last bout delivered an impressive batch of widespread heavy rainfall and scattered strong to severe thunderstorms. Large hail was common with severe storms across the eastern plains, particularly around De Baca, Roosevelt, and Curry counties where golf ball to tennis ball size hail was common. Heavy rainfall amounts between 2 and 4 inches were common as well across the area from Roosevelt to Curry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch accumulation of pea to nickel size hail.

A strong upper level low pressure area over western Arizona moved slowly eastward.  This feature increased upper level lift over West Texas and southeast New Mexico.  This upper low was pulling moisture northward into the area from the Gulf of Mexico allowing for precipitable water values to rise to around one inch.  Sufficient heating, instability, and high moisture content contributed to severe thunderstorms with large hail as well as flash flooding.  Thunderstorms increased in coverage overnight as an upper level disturbance moved over the region.

An upper level low pressure system that slowly moved southward through California provided deep southerly flow over New Mexico. Subtropical moisture surged northward ahead of this system in several waves over New Mexico. The first wave of moisture and instability moved over the area early on October 19th and created severe thunderstorms from the predawn hours through the late morning. A couple inches of severe hail moved over Socorro and impacted travel along Interstate 25. Storms continued to develop through the morning and impacted areas of Valencia and Chaves counties with mainly nickel to quarter sized hail. Another round of thunderstorms with large hail impacted the region later in the day around Los Alamos, Espanola, and parts of the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Picture forwarded through local media showing dime to nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The slow-moving upper level low pressure system that produced severe weather and flooding on the 4th and 5th delivered a deluge to eastern New Mexico on the 7th. A deep tap of tropical moisture surged northeast ahead the upper low and resulted in an extensive area of thunderstorms with torrential rainfall over southeastern New Mexico. The heaviest rain impacted the area around Clovis and Portales where upwards of 2 to 4 inches were reported. Several streets and state highways were closed due to flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail near Tolar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary across far northeast New Mexico allowed for multiple storms to generate and move southeast off Raton Mesa.  Storm activity began by the late afternoon on the 18th and continued through the evening hours as severe storms trained over similar areas. Four different rounds of hail fell in Capulin according to a COOP observer, with baseball size hail being the largest stone observed. Hayden, New Mexico also experienced baseball size hail as severe storms reached the southeast portions of Union county.  This event produced multiple reports of hail damage to cars, broken skylights and car windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain through the afternoon of the 21st and moved to the southeast.  Aided by a strong mid-level jet and a drier environment, storms were able to produce strong outflow winds with only spotty rain.  The strongest wind gust recorded was 56 mph within the southwest mountains, though no damage was reported.  A strong outflow from a dying thunderstorm overturned a mobile home being transported along U.S. 285 west of Dexter. Although local estimates of the speed of the gust are unknown, this outflow did produce a 49 mph wind gust not far from the area at the Roswell airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter passed along pictures of half a mobile home that was overturned near Dexter as it was being transported to Alto. The mobile home became completely detached from the towing truck, resulting in damage to siding and roof.  This was a high-profile vehicle traveling along U.S. Highway 285.  The other half avoided any issues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent strong westerly flow aloft resulted in high winds in the Guadalupe Mountains.

An upper level wave crossed over the Four Corners region and focused isolated severe thunderstorms over San Juan County during the evening of the 26th. A rapidly developing thunderstorm around Farmington moved quickly northeast and pounded the area from Flora Vista to Aztec shortly after sunset. These storms produced damage to trees and power lines, as well as flash flooding that damaged roads and businesses in the Aztec and Flora Vista areas. The flood waters pushed several homes off their foundations and swept away one propane gas tank. Several schools were impacted by flooding. Police and fire departments responded to 59 fire calls and 51 law enforcement calls about flooding, trapped individuals, power pole fires, and downed trees. Shelters were set up in the area for those impacted by flood waters, though no one had used the facilities. Sandbags were utilized to divert flood waters away from the nearby convention center. The flooding caused all schools to close in Aztec on the 27th and just the Lydia Rippey Elementary school on the 28th.  Major damages were also seen at the Aztec Municipal Golf Course at Hidden Valley, which closed until further notice for necessary repairs. Total estimated damages from this isolated severe thunderstorm were in excess of $1 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds caused multiple trees to fall over, landing on houses and power lines.  This caused power outages for portions of Aztec, Flora Vista, and Crouch Mesa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved slowly over portions of northern and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening of the 1st. The strongest of these storms rolled through the Santa Rosa area, dropping quarter size hail and producing damaging wind gusts up to 60 mph that caused roof damage to several buildings. The 1.5 to 2 inches of rain that accompanied the storm also caused flash flooding that resulted in one road being closed due to being submerged in water.  Between 1.5 and 3.35 inches of heavy rain also fell near Santa Fe, leading to flash flood waters between 1 and 1.5 feet high making a road impassable. No other significant flooding as a result of heavy rains was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public weather station reported 3.35 inches of rainfall in 1 hour near Las Campanas. Video showed flash flood waters flowing quickly over East Sunrise Road northwest of Santa Fe, making it impassable. The water depth was reported to be between 1 and 1.5 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave crossed over the Four Corners region and focused isolated severe thunderstorms over San Juan County during the evening of the 26th. A rapidly developing thunderstorm around Farmington moved quickly northeast and pounded the area from Flora Vista to Aztec shortly after sunset. These storms produced damage to trees and power lines, as well as flash flooding that damaged roads and businesses in the Aztec and Flora Vista areas. Police and fire departments responded to 59 fire calls and 51 law enforcement calls about flooding, trapped individuals, power pole fires, and downed trees. Shelters were set up in the area for those impacted by flood waters, though no one had used the facilities. Sandbags were utilized to divert flood waters away from the nearby convention center. The flooding caused all schools to close in Aztec on the 27th and just the Lydia Rippey Elementary school on the 28th.  Major damages were also seen at the Aztec Municipal Golf Course at Hidden Valley, which closed until further notice for necessary repairs. Total estimated damages from this isolated severe thunderstorm were in excess of $1 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant damage reported on a property near Flora Vista likely the result of intense microburst winds.  The winds downed over 100 very large cottonwood trees, including a few that were completely uprooted.  The tops of a few trees were completely snapped off, as well.  Metal roofing on a barn was torn off and horizontally displaced about 20 yards on nearby power poles.  The falling timber along with the wind itself caused damage to a barn roof, a pop up camper, a canoe, a trampoline, and a camper. Based on the EF-toolkit winds from this storm may have reached as high as 104 mph. No injuries or further damages resulted from the microburst.

Total estimated damages from this isolated severe thunderstorm were in excess of $1 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blanco Arroyo overflowed its banks and, combined with heavy rain across the area, led to significant flash flooding. Firefighters rescued a few individuals from the roofs of their homes, otherwise no major injuries or fatalities were reported.  Several homes were reportedly shifted off their foundation and a propane tank at one of those homes was swept away in the flood waters.  Multiple classrooms were damaged at three area schools, causing all schools to be closed on the 27th and one school, Lydia Rippey Elementary, to be closed on the 28th.  The Aztec Municipal Golf Course at Hidden Valley was closed until further notice due to damages incurred from the flooding.  A large rock slide closed Navajo Road 36 at mile marker 28, with other smaller road closures reported around Aztec.  Measured rainfall in the area ranges from an inch to a little over an inch and a half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high centered over far southeast New Mexico on the 2nd allowed the monsoon moisture plume to focus over much of the state. The subsequent afternoon and evening thunderstorms were slow-moving and, at times, strong to severe, producing areas of heavy rain, hail, and strong winds. Flash flooding was reported in the northwest and south central portions of the state, impacting U.S. Highway 550 near Nageezi and U.S. Highway 60 west of Socorro. Penny size hail fell with stronger storms that rolled through Tucumcari during the early evening hours.  No other severe weather was reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highway 60 closed due to high water at mile marker 124.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to penny size hail in Tucumcari. Lightning strike also blew up a transformer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-defined moisture plume surged back east over New Mexico as an upper level trough migrated eastward into California. Scattered showers and thunderstorms that developed along the Continental Divide battled the low level dry air from the previous several days. A storm crossed the area around El Morro and produced a severe downburst wind along State Road 53. Spotters estimated wind speeds topping well over 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported an intense burst of wind blew over several light weight objects on the property, including lawn furniture and a dog house. The dog was not injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of slow-moving thunderstorms developed between Cannon Air Force Base and Clovis during the late afternoon hours. Wet microburst winds and hail slammed Clovis before organizing into an extended line of torrential rainfall from northern Roosevelt County through much of Curry County. Emergency management reported near zero visibility in heavy rain. Fierce lightning struck a duplex home and resulted in a large blaze that severely damaged the home. Nickel to penny size hail was also reported around the city. Minor flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind of 62 mph measured.

A few storms became severe and produced pea to quarter size hail along with damaging winds, including an estimated wind gust of 68 mph near Clayton.  The strong winds resulted in damaged weather equipment, nat least two downed trees, and multiple road signs blown onto roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roswell airport ASOS recorded a thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front pushed through the eastern plains during the afternoon and evening of the 10th, enhancing thunderstorm development along and east of the central mountains.  Storms coalesced around east central New Mexico and continued moving slowly to the east, dropping heavy rain and occasional hail.  The largest hail reported was quarter size near Logan, an area that also received 5.05 inches of rain throughout the event.  The heavy rains produced flash flooding along U.S. Highway 54 between Corona and Carrizozo,  causing the road to be closed for 45 minutes. Additional reports of minor flooding were noted in Fort Sumner, Cedarvale, Santa Fe and Ruidoso.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported near Logan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage along Galisteo Creek below Galisteo rose to above minor flood stage at 1 am on the 11th due to rains late on the 10th. It peaked above moderate flood stage around by 330am before dropping below moderate flood stage around 515am, and completely below minor flood stage at 730am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary boundary over far northeast New Mexico produced thunderstorms with heavy rain and large hail. Half dollar size hail was reported from one severe storm in Folsom and another storm over the Capulin National Monument. Radar estimated between 2 to 4 inches of rain were common in northern Union county, causing a rise in the Dry Cimarron River above action stage at Kenton, OK. It also caused a mud slide along a mile-long stretch of roadway that leads to the top of the Capulin National Monument, with boulders up to 55 pounds in size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail fell briefly in Folsom.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Staff living near the Capulin National Monument reported hail just smaller than ping pong size with storm that hit during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the afternoon and moved slowly to the southeast as the upper level high remained centered over western New Mexico.  Weak upslope surface flow aided thunderstorm production and allowed for a few storms to become severe in the northeast highlands near Maxwell. The storm produced pea to quarter size hail while lightning knocked out power in the area.  A public report indicated the storm dropped nearly 2.5 inches of rain as well.  No other damage or flooding was reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter size hail fell in Maxwell while lightning knocked out power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused a mud slide along a mile stretch of roadway that leads to the top of the Capulin National Monument.  The debris included mud and boulders that were up to 55 pounds in weight.  The road remained closed into the afternoon on the 14th as staff attempted to clear the debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure was centered over the Arizona and New Mexico border by the afternoon of the 14th, with northwest flow moving over the northeast portion of New Mexico. Thunderstorms developed along the central mountain chain and moved southeast, being aided by southeasterly surface winds.  Two separate thunderstorm complexes developed and moved across the eastern plains, one during the mid afternoon hours and during the late night into the early morning hours on the 16th. One storm complex produced pea to nickel size hail and strong winds in San Jose, while another dropped over 2 inches of rain with 60 mph winds near the Melrose Gunnery Range.  No damage resulted from the hail and winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel size hail fell along with an estimated 45 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave within northwest flow aloft traversed northern New Mexico during the afternoon of the 17th, helping to initiate areas of strong to severe storms.  Storm type was initially more multi-cellular with embedded supercells over the northern mountains and northeast highlands before slowly transitioning to more of a squall line-like feature as it entered the east central plains during the evening.  The embedded supercell thunderstorms managed to produce multiple severe hail reports throughout the day, ranging from nickel to baseball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail fell along highway 285 south of Tres Piedras.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary across far northeast New Mexico allowed for multiple storms to generate and move southeast off Raton Mesa.  Storm activity began by the late afternoon on the 18th and continued through the evening hours as severe storms trained over similar areas. Four different rounds of hail fell in Capulin according to a COOP observer, with baseball size hail being the largest stone observed. Hayden, New Mexico also experienced baseball size hail as severe storms reached the southeast portions of Union county.  This event produced multiple reports of hail damage to cars, broken skylights and car windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and strong instability with northwest flow aloft over eastern New Mexico set the stage for yet another round of severe thunderstorms. This was the most significant round of severe weather to impact the area for the month to date. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on the afternoon of 8th and moved southeast into the high plains. A slow-moving severe thunderstorm near Wagon Mound dumped an impressive 1 foot of dime to quarter size hail. Union County was the epicenter for large hail where up to ping pong ball size hail and damaging winds were reported. Several other thunderstorms across east central and northeast New Mexico were also very strong, however no reports of severe weather were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported that large hail and strong winds caused 4 inch diameter tree limps to snap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was good moisture across southeast New Mexico and the Trans Pecos. This moisture along with daytime heating created a good environment for thunderstorms to develop as a frontal boundary moved into the area through the evening hours.  Thunderstorms developed along and behind this frontal boundary that produced hail and damaging winds.

An upper level high centered over far southeast New Mexico on the 2nd allowed the monsoon moisture plume to focus over much of the state. The subsequent afternoon and evening thunderstorms were slow-moving and, at times, strong to severe, producing areas of heavy rain, hail, and strong winds. Flash flooding was reported in the northwest and south central portions of the state, impacting U.S. Highway 550 near Nageezi and U.S. Highway 60 west of Socorro. Penny size hail fell with stronger storms that rolled through Tucumcari during the early evening hours.  No other severe weather was reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of rapidly flowing water over U.S. Highway 550 at mile maker 117. Nearby arroyo was raging with flash flood waters out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front pushed through the eastern plains during the afternoon and evening of the 10th, enhancing thunderstorm development along and east of the central mountains.  Storms coalesced around east central New Mexico and continued moving slowly to the east, dropping heavy rain and occasional hail.  The largest hail reported was quarter size near Logan, an area that also received 5.05 inches of rain throughout the event.  The heavy rains produced flash flooding along U.S. Highway 54 between Corona and Carrizozo,  causing the road to be closed for 45 minutes. Additional reports of minor flooding were noted in Fort Sumner, Cedarvale, Santa Fe and Ruidoso.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Mexico Department of Transportation closed U.S. highway 54 at mile markers 124 and 170 due to heavy rain and difficult driving conditions.  This closure lasted 45 minutes and prevented traffic to drive between Corona and Carrizozo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough was moving through the central and southern plains with a back door cold front located near the Rio Grande Valley. A severe thunderstorm developed along this boundary and produced up to one inch diameter hail in the Las Cruces area.
There was a surface trough in southeast New Mexico with plentiful surface heating.  This contributed to instability and allowed storms with strong winds and large hail to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm moved across Lea County and produced wind damage near Knowles.  A trained spotter reported several power poles blown down just west of NM 132 and NM 133.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms drifted northeast across far northeastern New Mexico. These storms produced several rounds of hail around Raton and numerous funnel clouds from Raton to near Des Moines. One particularly strong storm produced accumulations of dime to penny size hail in Raton. Heavy rainfall was also reported with this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail accumulated several inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved slowly over portions of northern and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening of the 1st. The strongest of these storms rolled through the Santa Rosa area, dropping quarter size hail and producing damaging wind gusts up to 60 mph that caused roof damage to several buildings. The 1.5 to 2 inches of rain that accompanied the storm also caused flash flooding that resulted in one road being closed due to being submerged in water.  Between 1.5 and 3.35 inches of heavy rain also fell near Santa Fe, leading to flash flood waters between 1 and 1.5 feet high making a road impassable. No other significant flooding as a result of heavy rains was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported an estimated wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of slow-moving thunderstorms developed between Cannon Air Force Base and Clovis during the late afternoon hours. Wet microburst winds and hail slammed Clovis before organizing into an extended line of torrential rainfall from northern Roosevelt County through much of Curry County. Emergency management reported near zero visibility in heavy rain. Fierce lightning struck a duplex home and resulted in a large blaze that severely damaged the home. Nickel to penny size hail was also reported around the city. Minor flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cannon Air Force Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave within northwest flow aloft traversed northern New Mexico during the afternoon of the 17th, helping to initiate areas of strong to severe storms.  Storm type was initially more multi-cellular with embedded supercells over the northern mountains and northeast highlands before slowly transitioning to more of a squall line-like feature as it entered the east central plains during the evening.  The embedded supercell thunderstorms managed to produce multiple severe hail reports throughout the day, ranging from nickel to baseball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter size hail with 50 mph estimated wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An enhanced jet stream created enough lift and destabilization along with just enough moisture for isolated showers and thunderstorms across western and northern New Mexico. Significant dry air below the convection limited rain potential but allowed for a few storms to produce strong microburst outflow winds.  One such microburst hit the southern valley portion of Albuquerque, causing a tramopline to be lifted on top of a power pole.  Other notable reported microbursts include a 58 mph wind gust at the Roswell International Airport, a 56 mph wind gust at the Taos airport, and a 55 mph wind gust in Moriarty.  No other damages or impacts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media member supplied pictures of trampoline that was blown on top of a power line, indicating an estimated wind speed of 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak backdoor cold front edged into northeast New Mexico early on the 29th and provided an area of upslope flow on the southern tier of the Rocky Mountains. A small disturbance within northwest flow aloft supported the initiation of thunderstorms during the early afternoon, moving them to swiftly to the southeast through the early evening hours.  A few became strong to severe in northeast New Mexico, with one storm producing half dollar size hail.  No significant damage resulted from the hail.

A subtropical ridge was centered over northern Mexico with an upper level trough over the Intermountain West.  There was good low level moisture, upslope winds, and instability present across southeast New Mexico and West Texas.  There was a difference of about 30 to 40 degrees between the temperature and dewpoint temperature which allowed for thunderstorms with gusty winds to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Eddy County and produced a 53 knot gust near Carlsbad.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a weakened subtropical ridge in place with good moisture, intense heating, and upslope winds present across southeast New Mexico.  A lee surface trough was also present across the plains of southeast New Mexico.  These conditions resulted in thunderstorms with gusty winds developing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Eddy County and produced an estimated 60 mph wind gust in Carlsbad.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant tropical moisture moved into New Mexico on the 21st but lack of heating limited significant rainfall rates and impacts.  More heating, more instability and plenty of moisture on the 22nd led to scattered thunderstorms across the state.  A few of these storms produced wet microbursts that resulted in significant flooding and wind damage. A series of storms hit the Albuquerque metro area through the afternoon, causing lots of street flooding and uprooting a few trees. One person died as a result of strong flows within the arroyo channels despite best rescue efforts. Flash flooding was also reported just north of Roswell, where reported rainfall amounts exceeded 3 inches over just a two hour span.  There were reports of a few swift water rescues with no injuries or deaths along with many roads being closed. The 2.45 inches reported at a COOP site north of Roswell was the largest single day rainfall total in the month of September since the historic flash flood event of 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported widespread street flooding in Roswell.  Later reports and information showed road closures in the northeast portion of the city near the Anderson Museum of Contemporary Art, notably North Virginia Road and North Garden Road.  There were unconfirmed reports of swift water rescues in the area as well.  Rainfall reports from trained spotters and the CoCoRaHS network around the area showed up to 3.68 inches of rain fell, with many reports in excess of 2.5 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reports indicated a man was swept away and died in fast moving water that filled the north diversion channel arroyo.  Eye witness reports noted the man was outside the arroyo before somehow ending up in it and being swept away.  Search and rescue attempts by the fire department found the body two hours later in the Rio Grande.  The only other information available at this time is that it was a male between the ages of 20 and 30 years old. The intense rainfall rates also washed lots of landscaping material, including rocks, mud, and fabric material, into streets across the metro area. A water mark near Carlisle and Comanche suggested the water was as high as 2 feet in the intersection. Road crews spent several hours cleaning up streets across the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A saggy upper level low combined with diurnal heating to generate isolated severe thunderstorms across the eastern plains of New Mexico during the afternoon of the 23rd.  Nickel to quarter size hail was reported with storms to the west of Roswell and to the southeast of Tucumcari, but no damage was reported.  Aided by remnant tropical moisture from the previous days, these storms also produced very heavy rain that prompted flash flood warnings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of moist, unstable southeast low level flow beneath dry and fast northwest flow aloft created an environment for isolated severe thunderstorms over portions of eastern New Mexico. Thunderstorms developed early in the afternoon over the central mountain chain and moved slowly southeast onto the high plains. Several storms quickly became strong then eventually severe with large hail and high winds. Quarter size hail was reported around Edgewood during the early afternoon. Another storm produced a severe wind gust near Fort Sumner. This activity merged into a large cluster of slow-moving storms around Lincoln County later in the afternoon where localized flash flooding impacted state road 462.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused state road 462 to be closed by the New Mexico DOT between mile markers 0 and 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was an upper level ridge centered over the Four Corners region.  A surface boundary was present across the Permian Basin.  The atmosphere was moist and unstable which allowed for storms with flash flooding to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain moved across Lea County and produced flash flooding in Hobbs.  Local law enforcement reported that Highway 18 was flooded and smaller streets throughout Hobbs were flooded.  At least two cars were reported to be stranded.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET/RED RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep layer of sub-tropical moisture that surged northeast off the eastern Pacific Ocean interacted with a shallow but potent cold airmass over eastern New Mexico around the Thanksgiving holiday. A persistent band of freezing rain moved over the area from Lincoln County northeast across the eastern plains of New Mexico and into west Texas. A rare freezing rain event resulted in severe impacts to travel across much of this area, including Interstate 40 and U.S. Highways 54, 285, 60/84, and 70/380. Widespread freezing rain accumulations of one quarter inch to one half inch were reported, with locally higher amounts. Light snow accumulations fell on top of the ice in a few areas mainly north of Interstate 40. Power lines were downed around Curry County. Numerous traffic accidents occurred over many areas of eastern New Mexico as a result of the icy travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snowfall was reported around the high terrain. Minor impacts to travel were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system that rapidly strengthened just west of the Four Corners on November 15th produced strong winds and widespread precipitation across the area through the 17th, including 1 to 3 feet of snow for the central and northern mountains and parts of northeastern New Mexico. Blizzard conditions developed for several hours between Raton and Clayton early on the 17th forcing the closure of I-25 between Las Vegas and Raton Pass. Treacherous travel conditions were also reported across other areas of the northeast including U.S. 64/87 between Raton and Clayton, where snow drifts between 2 and 3 feet were reported. This was the first significant snowfall event of this winter season for lower elevation areas from Taos to Espanola, Santa Fe, and the east mountain communities. Numerous school closures and delays were noted across the state on the 16th and the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP site reported 4 inches of snow around Dulce. Hazardous travel conditions were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and SNOTEL reports across the area indicated between 10 and 18 inches of snowfall with hazardous travel conditions in blowing snow. Phone services were disrupted as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low dove south out of the Great Basin into Northern Mexico. A cold front pushed in from the northeast and a second one from the west which brought sub-freezing temperatures to the region and a nice tap of moisture. As the storm peaked during the overnight hours, blizzard conditions with wind gusts up to 90 mph were reported in the Sacramento Mountains which brought down trees and power lines. Snowfall amounts were up to 30 inches in the Cloudcroft area with 6 to 12 inches common over the lowlands of New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across Luna County ranged from 4 to 8 inches with the highest report coming from 11 miles south-southeast of Deming.
